content: The population $p$ of the city at time $t$ is given by $\frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100$. If initial population is 100 then $\mathrm{p}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100}=\mathrm{dt} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \log \left|\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100\right|=\mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{t}}{2}} \mathrm{c} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

where $\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{c}_1}{2}}$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{p}=100$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{100}{2}-100=e^0 c \\ & \Rightarrow c=-50 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{p}}{2}-100=-50 \mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{2}}\,\,\,\,\,\, ...[From (i)] \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=200-100 \mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{array}\\ &\text {} \end{aligned} $$

content: The solution of the equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{x+y+1}$ is

options.content: $x=\log (x+y+2)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$x=\log (x+y-2)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=\log (x+y+2)+c$, where $c$ is the constant of integration
$y=\log (x+y-2)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{x+y+1} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Put } x+y+1=v \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}-1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{v}+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v+1}{v} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}+1} \mathrm{dv}=\int \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}+1}\right) \mathrm{dv}=\int \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}-\log (\mathrm{v}+1)=x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y+1-\log (x+y+2)=x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow y=\log (x+y+2)+\mathrm{c}, \text { where } \mathrm{c}=\mathrm{c}_1-1 \end{aligned} $$

content: The solution of $\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=2 x-5 y, y(0)=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2 x-5 y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{2 x-5 y} \\ & \Rightarrow e^{5 y} d y=e^{2 x} d x \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{e}^{5 y}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 x}}{2}+\mathrm{c}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\ & y(0)=0 \text { i.e. when } x=0, y=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{2}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{-3}{10} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{e}^{5 y}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 x}}{2}-\frac{3}{10}\,\,\,\, \ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{e}^{2 x}-2 \mathrm{e}^{5 y}=3 \end{array} $$

content: The integrating factor of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \log x=x \cdot \mathrm{e}^x x^{-\frac{1}{2}} \log x(x>0)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \quad x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \log x=x \cdot \mathrm{e}^x x^{-\frac{1}{2}} \log x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{\log x}{x} \cdot y=\mathrm{e}^x x^{-\frac{1}{2}} \log x \\ & \therefore \quad \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\log x}{x} \mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{2}(\log x)^2} \\ & \quad=\left(\mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{2} \log x}\right)^{\log x} \quad \ldots\left[\because\left(\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{m}}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{mn}}\right] \\ & \quad=(\sqrt{x})^{\log x} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the curve passing through the point $(0,2)$ given that the sum of the ordinate and abscissa of any point exceeds the slope of the tangent to the curve at that point by 5 is
explanation:
The slope of the tangent at point $(x, y)$ is $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$.

According to the given condition,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+5=x+y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y=(x-5) \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int-1 . \mathrm{d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{-x} \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Solution of the given equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y \mathrm{e}^{-x}=\int(x-5) \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ &=\int x \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x-5 \int \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ &=-x \mathrm{e}^{-x}+\int \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+5 \mathrm{e}^{-x}+\mathrm{c} \\ &=-x \mathrm{e}^{-x}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-x}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad y \mathrm{e}^{-x}=-x \mathrm{e}^{-x}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-x}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \quad y=-x+4+c \mathrm{e}^x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

But the required curve passes through $(0,2)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2=0+4+c \mathrm{e}^0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=-2 \\ \therefore & y=4-x-2 \mathrm{e}^x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\end{array} $$

content: The solution of the differential equation $(1+x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-x y=1-x$ is

options.content: $y(1+x)=x+\mathrm{ce}^x$, where c is the constant of integration
$\quad y(1+x)=\mathrm{ce}^x$, where c is the constant of integration
$y(1-x)=x-\mathrm{ce}^x$, where c is the constant of integration
$y(1+x)=x$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & (1+x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-x y=1-x \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-\left(\frac{x}{1+x}\right) y=\frac{1-x}{1+x} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I. F. }=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{x}{1+x} \mathrm{dx}} \\ = & \mathrm{e}^{\int\left(-1+\frac{1}{1+x}\right) \mathrm{dx}} \\ = & \mathrm{e}^{-x+\log (1+x)} \\ = & \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{e}^{\log (1+x)} \\ = & \mathrm{e}^{-x}(1+x) \end{array}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Solution of the given equation is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x}=\int\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right)(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x}=\int(1-x) \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x}=\int \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x-\int x \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x}=-\mathrm{e}^{-x}-\left(-x \mathrm{e}^{-x}-\mathrm{e}^{-x}\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+x) \mathrm{e}^{-x}=x \mathrm{e}^{-x}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y(1+x)=x+c \mathrm{e}^x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The differential equation representing the family of parabolas having vertex at the origin and axis along the positive Y -axis is
explanation:
The equation of the family of parabolas with vertex at the origin and axis along the positive Y -axis is $x^2=4 \mathrm{a} y,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

where $\mathrm{a}>0$

Differentiating (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 x=4 a \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow a=\frac{x}{2} \frac{d x}{d y} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Substituting (ii) in (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2=4\left(\frac{x}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}\right) y \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-2 y=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The population of towns A and B increase at the rate proportional to their population present at that time. At the end of the year 1984, the population of both the towns was 20,000 . At the end of the year 1989, the population of town A was 25,000 and that of town B was 28,000 . The difference of populations of towns A and B at the end of 1994 was
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}$ be the populations of town A and town $B$ at time $t$ years, respectively.

Then, $\frac{d P_A}{d t}=k_1 P_A$ and $\frac{d P_B}{d t}=k_2 P_B$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\mathrm{k}_1 \mathrm{dt}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{dP}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\mathrm{k}_2 \mathrm{dt}$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$\log P_A=k_1 t+c_1$ and

$\log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{k}_2 \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}_2$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=20,000$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log 20000=\mathrm{c}_1 \text { and } \\ \therefore \quad & \log 20000=\mathrm{c}_2 \\ & \log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{k}_1 \mathrm{t}+\log 20000 \text { and } \\ & \log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{k}_2 \mathrm{t}+\log 20000 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{20000}\right)=\mathrm{k}_1 \mathrm{t} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { and } \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}{20000}\right)=\mathrm{k}_2 \mathrm{t} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

Town A:

When $\mathrm{t}=1989-1984=5, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=25,000$

$$ \therefore \quad \log \left(\frac{25000}{20000}\right)=5 \mathrm{k}_1 $$         …[From (i)]

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow k_1=\frac{1}{5} \log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{P_A}{20000}\right)=\frac{t}{5} \log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right)\,\,\,\, …[From (i)] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { When } t=1994-1984=10 \\ & \log \left(\frac{P_A}{20000}\right)=\frac{10}{5} \log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{P_A}{20000}\right)=2 \log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{P_A}{20000}=\frac{25}{16} \\ & \Rightarrow P_A=31,250 \end{aligned} $$

Town B:

When $\mathrm{t}=1989-1984=5, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=28,000$

$$ \therefore \quad \log \left(\frac{28000}{20000}\right)=5 \mathrm{k}_2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,. . .[\text { From (ii)] } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_2=\frac{1}{5} \log \left(\frac{7}{5}\right) \\ \therefore & \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}{20000}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{5} \log \left(\frac{7}{5}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,. . .[\text { From (ii)] }\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { When } t=1994-1984=10 \\ & \log \left(\frac{P_B}{20000}\right)=\frac{10}{5} \log \left(\frac{7}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{P_B}{20000}\right)=2 \log \left(\frac{7}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{P_B}{20000}=\frac{49}{25} \\ & \Rightarrow P_B=39,200 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Difference of populations }\\ &=39200-31250=7950 \end{aligned} $$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\cot x \cdot \cot y$ is

options.content: $\cos x=\mathrm{c} \operatorname{cosec} y$, where c is the constant of integration.
$\sin x=\mathrm{c} \sec y$, where c is the constant of integration.
$\sin x=x \cos y$, where c is the constant of integration.
$\cos x=\mathrm{c} \sin y$, where c is the constant of integration.

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\cot x \cot y \\ & \Rightarrow \cot x \mathrm{~d} x-\tan y \mathrm{~d} y=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \log (\sin x)-\log (\sec y)=\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\sin x}{\sec y}\right)=\log c \Rightarrow \sin x=c \sec y \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of a curve passing through $(1,0)$ and having slope of tangent at any point $(x, y)$ of the curve as $\frac{y-1}{x^2+x}$ is
explanation:
Given that $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y-1}{x^2+x}$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y-1}=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x(x+1)} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y-1}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x(x+1)} \\ & \Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y-1}=\int\left(\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x+1}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y-1)=\log x-\log (x+1)+\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y-1)=\log \left(\frac{x \mathrm{c}}{x+1}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y-1=\frac{x \mathrm{c}}{x+1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

Since the curve passes through $(1,0)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 0-1=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=-2 \\ \therefore & y-1=\frac{-2 x}{x+1}\,\,\,…[From (i)] \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x+(x+1)(y-1)=0 \end{array} $$

content: The differential equation which represents the family of curves $y=c_1 e^{c_2 x}$, where $c_1, c_2$ are arbitrary constants is
explanation:
$$ y=c_1 e^{c_2 x} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & y^{\prime}=c_1 c_2 e^{c_2 x} \\ & \Rightarrow y^{\prime}=c_2 y \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & y^{\prime \prime}=c_2 y^{\prime} \\ & \Rightarrow y^{\prime \prime}=\frac{\left(y^{\prime}\right)^2}{y} \ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \Rightarrow y y^{\prime \prime}=\left(y^{\prime}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate of increase of the population of a city is proportional to the population present at that instant. In the period of 40 years the population increased from 30,000 to 40,000 . At any time t the population is $(a)(b)^{\frac{t}{40}}$. Then the values of $a$ and $b$ are respectively
explanation:
Let P be the population at time t years. Then,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d P}{d t}=k P \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d P}{P}=k d t \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$\log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}=30,000$

$\therefore \quad \log 30,000=0+c$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 30,000 \\ \therefore & \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\log 30,000 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{30000}\right)=\mathrm{kt} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=40, \mathrm{P}=40,000 \\ \therefore & \log \left(\frac{40000}{30000}\right)=40 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{40} \log \left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{30000}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{40} \log \left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\, ...[From (i)]\\ \quad & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{30000}\right)=\log \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{40}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=30,000\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{40}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=30,000, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{4}{3} \end{array}\\ &\text { } \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the curve passing through origin and satisfying $\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 x y=4 x^2$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=4 x^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right) y=\frac{4 x^2}{1+x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^2} \mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+x^2\right)}=1+x^2 \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Solution of the given equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y\left(1+x^2\right)=\int \frac{4 x^2}{1+x^2} \times\left(1+x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y\left(1+x^2\right)=\int 4 x^2 \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y\left(1+x^2\right)=\frac{4}{3} x^3+\mathrm{c} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Since the curve passes through origin $(0,0)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 0=\frac{4}{3}(0)+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=0 \\ \therefore & y\left(1+x^2\right)=\frac{4}{3} x^3\,\,\,\,\, …[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow 3 y\left(1+x^2\right)=4 x^3 \end{array} $$

content: The order of the differential equation whose general solution is given by $y=\left(\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2\right) \sin \left(x+\mathrm{C}_3\right)-\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{e}^{x+\mathrm{C}_5}$ is (where $\mathrm{C}_1, \mathrm{C}_2, \mathrm{C}_3, \mathrm{C}_4, \mathrm{C}_5$ are arbitrary constants)
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} y & =\left(C_1+C_2\right) \sin \left(x+C_3\right)-C_4 \mathrm{e}^{x+C_5} \\ & =\left(C_1+C_2\right) \sin \left(x+C_3\right)-C_4 \mathrm{e}^x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{C_5} \\ & =A \sin \left(x+C_3\right)-B \mathrm{e}^x \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad \ldots\left[A=C_1+C_2, B=C_4 \mathrm{e}^{C_5}\right] \end{aligned} $$

There are 3 arbitrary constants.

$$ \therefore $$     Order of the differential equation is 3 .

content:
If $x=\operatorname{sint}$ and $y=\sin p t$, then the value of

$$ \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{p}^2 y= $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x=\sin \mathrm{t} \text { and } y=\sin \mathrm{pt} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\cos \mathrm{t} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{p} \cos \mathrm{pt} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}}=\frac{\mathrm{p} \cos \mathrm{pt}}{\cos \mathrm{t}} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{p} \sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{array}\\ &\text { Again, differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{p} \sqrt{1-x^2}\left(\frac{-2 y}{2 \sqrt{1-y^2}}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-\mathrm{p} \sqrt{1-y^2}\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)}{\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=-\mathrm{p} y \frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{p} x \frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=-\mathrm{p}^2 y+x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \end{aligned}\\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ...[From (i)] } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{p}^2 y=0 $$

content: The general solution of differential equation $\left(y^2-x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x=x y \mathrm{~d} y(x \neq 0)$ is

options.content: $2 x^2 \log x+y^2+2 \mathrm{cx}^2=0$, where c is the constant of integration
$\quad 2 x^2 \log x-y^2+2 \mathrm{cx}^2=0$, where c is the constant of integration
$x^2 \log x+y^2+2 \mathrm{cx}^2=0$, where c is the constant of integration
$x^2 \log x-y^2+2 \mathrm{cx}^2=0$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \left(y^2-x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x=x y \mathrm{~d} y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y^2-x^2}{x y} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Put } y=\mathrm{v} x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned}\\ &\text { Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{(\mathrm{v} x)^2-x^2}{x(\mathrm{v} x)} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2-1}{\mathrm{v}} \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2-1}{\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v} \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-1}{\mathrm{v}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate at which the population of a city increases varies as the population. In a period of 20 years, the population increased from 4 lakhs to 6 lakhs. In another 20 years the population will be
explanation:
Let P be the population at time t years.

Then, $\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kP}$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{kdt} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \log P=k t+c $$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}=400000$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log 400000=0+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 400000 \\ \therefore & \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\log 400000 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{400000}\right)=\mathrm{kt} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{array} $$

When $t=20$ years, $P=600000$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{600000}{400000}\right)=20 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{20} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{400000}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{20} \log \frac{3}{2} \,\,\,\,\, ... [From (i)] \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Population after another 20 years i.e. $\mathrm{t}=40$ years,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{400000}\right)=\frac{40}{20} \log \frac{3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{400000}\right)=2 \log \frac{3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}}{400000}=\frac{9}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=9 \text { lakhs } \end{aligned} $$

content: The differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 y$ represents ________

options.content: a family of circles with radius c .
a family of parabolas with vertex at the origin and axis along the positive Y -axis
a family of parabolas with vertex at origin and axis along the positive X -axis

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{y}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~d} x}{x} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \log y=2 \log x+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \log y=\log x^2+\log c \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \log y=\log c x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow y=c x^2 \end{aligned} $$

The above equation represents a family of parabolas with vertex at the origin and axis along the Y -axis.

content: The solution of the equation $x^2 y-x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y^4 \cos x$, where $y(0)=1$, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & x^2 y-x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y^4 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow x^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-x^2 y=-y^4 \cos x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{y^4} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{1}{x y^3}=\frac{1}{x^3} \cos x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Put } \frac{1}{y^3}=\mathrm{v} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{y^4} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ \therefore & \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{\mathrm{v}}{x}=\frac{1}{x^3} \cos x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{3 \mathrm{v}}{x}=\frac{3}{x^3} \cos x \\ \therefore & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int_x^3 d x}=\mathrm{e}^{3 \log x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log x^3}=x^3 \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Solution of the given equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { v. } x^3=\int \frac{3}{x^3} \cos x \cdot x^3 d x+c \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^3}{y^3}=3 \int \cos x d x+c \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^3}{y^3}=3 \sin x+c \end{aligned} $$

$y(0)=1$ i.e. when $x=0, y=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{0}{1}=3 \sin 0+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad \frac{x^3}{y^3}=3 \sin x \\ \Rightarrow x^3=3 y^3 \sin x \end{array} $$

content: $y=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~A} \cos x+\mathrm{B} \sin x)$ is the solution of the differential equation
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \begin{array}{l} y=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~A} \cos x+\mathrm{B} \sin x) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~A} \cos x+\mathrm{B} \sin x) +\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~B} \cos x-\mathrm{A} \sin x) \end{array} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~B} \cos x-\mathrm{A} \sin x) \quad \ldots \text { (i) } \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~B} \cos x-\mathrm{A} \sin x)-\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~A} \cos x+\mathrm{B} \sin x) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y\right)-y \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate of change of volume of spherical balloon at any instant is directly proportional to its surface area. If initially its radius is 3 cm , after 2 minutes its radius becomes 9 cm , then radius of balloon after 4 minutes is
explanation:
Let the volume and surface area of the spherical balloon be V and A respectively

Given that, $\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \mathrm{A}$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kA} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3\right)=\mathrm{k} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \times \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{dr}=\mathrm{kdt} \end{array} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{r}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$ \Rightarrow c=3 $$

When $\mathrm{t}=2$ minutes, $\mathrm{r}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$

$\therefore \quad$ From (i)

$$ \begin{aligned} & 9=2 k+c \\ & \Rightarrow 9=2 k+3 \\ & \Rightarrow k=3 \\\therefore & \,\,\, r=3 t+3 \end{aligned} $$

When $\mathrm{t}=4$ minutes,

$$ \mathrm{r}=3 \times 4+3=15 \mathrm{~cm} $$

content: Solution of $(2 y-x) \frac{d y}{d x}=1$ is

options.content: $x=2(y-1)+\mathrm{ce}^{-y}$, where c is the constant of integration
$x=2(y-1)+\mathrm{ce}^{-x}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=2(x-1)+\mathrm{ce}^{-x}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=2(x-1)+\mathrm{ce}^{-y}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & (2 y-x) \frac{d y}{d x}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y-x=\frac{d x}{d y} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+x=2 y \end{aligned} $$

Here, $\mathrm{P}=1, \mathrm{Q}=2 y$

$$ \text { I.F. }=e^{\int 1 d y}=e^y $$

$\therefore \quad$ Solution of the given equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & x \times \text { (I. F.) }=\int \mathrm{Q} \text { (I.F.) } \mathrm{d} y+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^y=\int 2 y \mathrm{e}^y \mathrm{~d} y+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2\left[y \int \mathrm{e}^y \mathrm{~d} y-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \times \int \mathrm{e}^y \mathrm{~d} y\right) \mathrm{d} y\right]+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 y \mathrm{e}^y-2 \mathrm{e}^y+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^y=2 \mathrm{e}^y(y-1)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow x=2(y-1)+\mathrm{ce}^{-y} \end{aligned} $$

content: The integrating factor of $y+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}(x y)=x(\sin x+\log x)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & y+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}(x y)=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & \Rightarrow y+\left(x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y\right)=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{2}{x} y=\sin x+\log x \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { This is of form } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{P}(x) y=\mathrm{Q}(x)\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { I.F. } & =\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2}{x} d x} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{2 \log x} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\log x^2}=x^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The differential equation whose solution is $\mathrm{A} x^2+\mathrm{B} y^2=1$, where A and B are arbitrary constants is of
explanation:
$$ \mathrm{A} x^2+\mathrm{B} y^2=1 $$

Differentiating w.r.t $x$, we get,

$$ A x+B y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Again differentiating w.r.t $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad A+B y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+B\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{B y}{x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+B y \frac{d^2 y}{d x}+B\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,. . .[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \Rightarrow x y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2-y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Order }=2, \text { Degree }=1 \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate at which a substance cools in moving air, is proportional to the difference between the temperature of the substance and that of air. The temperature of air is 290 K and the substance cools from 370 K to 330 K in 10 minutes. Then the time to cool the substance upto 295 K is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{T}_0$ be the air temperature and T be temperature of the substance at time $t$. Then, $\frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_0\right)$, where $\mathrm{k}>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}(\mathrm{~T}-290) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{~T}-290}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \log (\mathrm{T}-290)=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~T}=370 \\ &\therefore \log (370-290)=0+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 80 \\ &\therefore \log (\mathrm{~T}-290)=-\mathrm{kt}+\log 80 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=10, \mathrm{~T}=330 \\ & \therefore \quad \log (330-290)=-10 \mathrm{k}+\log 80 \\ & \Rightarrow \log 40=-10 \mathrm{k}+\log 80 \\ & \Rightarrow 10 \mathrm{k}=\log \frac{80}{40} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{10} \log 2 \\ & \therefore \quad \log (\mathrm{~T}-290)=\frac{-\mathrm{t}}{10} \log 2+\log 80 \end{aligned} $$

                                                                              …[From (i)]

When $T=295$, we have

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log (295-290)=\frac{-t}{10} \log 2+\log 80 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{t}{10} \log 2=\log \frac{80}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{t}{10} \log 2=\log 16 \\ & \Rightarrow t=10 \frac{\log 16}{\log 2} \\ & \quad=10 \frac{\log 2^4}{\log 2}=40 \mathrm{~min} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y(\log y-\log x+1)$, then the solution of the equation is

options.content: $\log \frac{x}{y}=\mathrm{c} y$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \frac{y}{x}=\mathrm{c} y$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \frac{x}{y}=\mathrm{c} x$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \frac{y}{x}=\mathrm{cx}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y(\log y-\log x+1) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y}{x}\left[\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+1\right] \\ & \text { Put } \mathrm{v}=\frac{y}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & y=\mathrm{v} x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { From (i), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}(\log \mathrm{v}+1) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v} \\ & \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{1}{v \log v} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \log (\log v)=\log x+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \log (\log v)=\log x+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \log (\log v)=\log (x c) \\ & \Rightarrow \log v=x c \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The order and degree of the differential equation $\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}-4 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-7 x=0$ is respectively
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}-4 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-7 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}\right)^2=\left(4 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+7 x\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=16\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2+56 x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+49 x^2 \end{aligned} $$

This is a differential equation of order 1 and degree 2 .

content: The differential equation of all circles touching the Y -axis at the origin and centre on the X -axis is
explanation:
Since the centres of all circles lie on the X-axis.

Let $(\mathrm{h}, 0)$ be the centre of the circle.

Radius of the circle $=\mathrm{h}$

Then, the equation of the circle is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad(x-\mathrm{h})^2+(y-0)^2=\mathrm{h}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad x^2-2 x \mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{h}^2+y^2=\mathrm{h}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad x^2-2 x \mathrm{~h}+y^2=0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 2 x-2 \mathrm{~h}+2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~h}=x+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

Substituting (ii) in (i), we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & x^2-2 x\left(x+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)+y^2=0 \\ \therefore & x^2-2 x^2-2 x y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y^2=0 \\ \therefore & x^2-y^2+2 x y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \end{array} $$

content: A wet substance in the open air loses its moisture at a rate proportional to the moisture content. If a sheet, hung in the open air, loses half its moisture during the first hour, then $90 \%$ of the moisture will be lost in ________ hours.
explanation:
Let the original amount of moisture in the substance be N , and the amount of moisture in the substance at any instant $t$ be $P$.

Then, $\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \mathrm{P}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{kP} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}}=-\int \mathrm{kdt} \\ \Rightarrow & \log \mathrm{P}=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{N} \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{c}=\log \mathrm{N} \\ \therefore \quad & \log \mathrm{P}=-\mathrm{kt}+\log \mathrm{N} \\ \Rightarrow & \log \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{~N}}=-\mathrm{kt}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=1, \mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N} \\ \therefore \quad & \log \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{2 \mathrm{~N}}=-\mathrm{k} \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{k}=\log 2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \log \frac{P}{N}=-t \log 2 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }]\\ &\text { Given that, } \mathrm{P}=(1-90 \%) \mathrm{N}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad=\frac{10}{100} \mathrm{~N}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~N} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{P}}=10 \\ & \text { Now, } \log \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{~N}}=-\mathrm{t} \log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{t} \log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \log 10=\mathrm{t} \log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{\log 10}{\log 2}=\log _2 10 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)$ is

options.content: $\log \tan \left(\frac{y}{2}\right)=\mathrm{c}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2}$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \tan \left(\frac{y}{4}\right)=\mathrm{c}-2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \left[\tan \left(\frac{y}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]=\mathrm{c}-2 \sin x$, where c is the constant of integration
$\log \left[\tan \left(\frac{y}{4}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]=\mathrm{c}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-2 \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y=-2 \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y=-2 \int \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \log \tan \left(\frac{y}{4}\right)=-4 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \tan \left(\frac{y}{4}\right)=\mathrm{c}-2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right), \text { where } \mathrm{c}=\frac{\mathrm{c}_1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the curve passing through $\left(2, \frac{9}{2}\right)$ and having the slope $\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)$ at $(x, y)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given that }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{1}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow d y=\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) d x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int \mathrm{d} y=\int\left(1-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \Rightarrow y=x+\frac{1}{x}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

Since the curve passes through $\left(2, \frac{9}{2}\right)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{9}{2}=2+\frac{1}{2}+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=2 \end{array} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the required curve is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=x+\frac{1}{x}+2 \\ & \Rightarrow x y=x^2+2 x+1 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=y(x)$ and $\left(\frac{2+\sin x}{y+1}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\cos x, y(0)=1$, then $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & \left(\frac{2+\sin x}{y+1}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\cos x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{y+1} \mathrm{~d} y=\frac{-\cos x}{2+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Integrating on both sides, we get } \\ & \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+\mathrm{c}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\ & \text { When } x=0, y=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{c}=2 \log 2=\log 4 \\ \therefore \quad & \begin{array}{l} \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+\log 4 \\ \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=\log \left(\frac{4}{2+\sin x}\right) \end{array} \\ \Rightarrow & y+1=\frac{4}{2+\sin x} \\ \Rightarrow & y=\frac{4}{2+\sin x}-1 \\ \therefore \quad & y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3} \end{array} $$

content: The degree of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+3\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=x^2 \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)$ is
explanation:
We are tasked with finding the degree of the differential equation:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} + 3\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 = x^2 \log\left(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right) $

Step 1: Recall the concept of degree of a differential equation

  • The degree of a differential equation is the power of the highest order derivative after the equation is made polynomial in all derivatives.

  • That means:

  1. There should be no transcendental (logarithmic, exponential, trigonometric, etc.) functions of derivatives.

  2. No fractional powers of derivatives.

If such functions appear in the differential equation and cannot be removed, the degree is not defined.

Step 2: Examine our equation

Highest order derivative: $\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$.

It appears in two places:

  • Linearly on the LHS: $\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$.

  • Inside a logarithm: $\log\left(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right)$.

Because the second derivative $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ appears inside $\log(\cdot)$, the equation is not a polynomial in the derivatives.

Step 3: Conclusion

Since we cannot remove the logarithmic involvement of the highest order derivative, the degree of the equation is not defined.

✅ Answer: Option D (Not defined)

content: If $y+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}(x y)=x(\sin x+\log x)$ then

options.content: $y=\cos x+\frac{2 \sin x}{x}+\frac{2}{x^2} \cos x+\frac{x}{3} \log x-\frac{x}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{x^2}$, where c is the constant of integration.
$y=-\cos x-\frac{2}{x} \sin x+\frac{2}{x^2} \cos x+\frac{x}{3} \log x-\frac{x}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{x^2}$ where c is the constant of integration.

$$ \begin{aligned} y=-\cos x+\frac{2}{x} \sin x+ & \frac{2}{x^2} \cos x +\frac{x}{3} \log x-\frac{x}{9}+\frac{c}{x^2}\text { where } \mathrm{c} \text { is the constant of integration. }\end{aligned} $$
$y=\cos x-\frac{2}{x} \sin x+\frac{2}{x^3} \cos x+\frac{x}{3} \log x-\frac{x}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{x^2}$ where $c$ is the constant of intergration.

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & y+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}(x y)=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & y+x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y=x(\sin x+\log x) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\sin x+\log x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\frac{2}{x}, \mathrm{Q}=\sin x+\log x \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { I.F } & =\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pd} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2}{x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{2 \log x} \\ & =x^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y \times \text { I.F }=\int \text { Q } \times \text { I.F } \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & y x^2=\int(\sin x+\log x) x^2 \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & y x^2=\int\left(x^2 \sin x+x^2 \log x\right) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & y x^2=\int x^2 \sin x \mathrm{~d} x+\int x^2 \log x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & =-x^2 \cos x-\int(2 x(-\cos x)) \mathrm{d} x +\left(\frac{x^3}{3}\right) \log x-\int\left(\frac{1}{x} \times \frac{x^3}{3}\right) \mathrm{dx} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =-x^2 \cos x+2\left[\int x \cos x \mathrm{~d} x\right]+\frac{x^3}{3} \log x-\frac{1}{3} \int x^2 \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & =-x^2 \cos x+2\left[x \sin x-\int(\sin x) \mathrm{d} x\right] +\frac{x^3}{3} \log x-\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{x^3}{3}\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Dividing by } x^2 \text {, we get }\\ &y=-\cos x+\frac{2 \sin x}{x}+\frac{2}{x^2} \cos x+\frac{x}{3} \log x-\frac{x}{9}+\frac{c}{x^2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The population of a town increases at a rate proportional to the population at that time. If the population increases from forty thousand to eighty thousand in 20 years, then the population in another 40 years will be
explanation:
Let P be the population at time t years.

$$ \frac{d p}{d t} \propto P $$

Then, $\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kP}$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{kdt} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} $$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}=40000$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log 40000=0+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 40000 \\ \therefore & \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\log 40000 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{40000}\right)=\mathrm{kt} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{array} $$

When $\mathrm{t}=20 \mathrm{yrs}, \mathrm{P}=80000$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{80000}{40000}\right)=20 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \log 2=20 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{\log 2}{20} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}}{40000}\right)=\frac{\log 2}{20} \mathrm{t}   \,\,\,\,\,\,\,    …[From (i)]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Population in another } 40 \text { years }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { When, } t=60 \\ & \log \left(\frac{P}{40000}\right)=\frac{\log 2}{20} \times 60 \\ & \log \left(\frac{P}{40000}\right)=3 \log 2 \\ & \frac{P}{40000}=8 \\ & P=3,20,000 \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: A particular solution of $3 \mathrm{e}^x \tan y \mathrm{~d} x+\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right) \sec ^2 y \mathrm{~d} y=0$ with $y(1)=\frac{\pi}{4}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } 3 \mathrm{e}^x \tan y \mathrm{~d} x+\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right) \sec ^2 y \mathrm{~d} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{e}^x \tan y \mathrm{~d} x=-\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right) \sec ^2 y \mathrm{~d} y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3 \mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{~d} x}{\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)}=-\frac{\sec ^2 y}{\tan y} \mathrm{~d} y \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we got }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{3 \mathrm{e}^x}{\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)} \mathrm{d} x=-\int \frac{\sec ^2 y}{\tan y} \mathrm{~d} y \\ & -3 \int \frac{-\mathrm{e}^x}{1-\mathrm{e}^x} \mathrm{~d} x=-\int \frac{\sec ^2 y}{\tan y} \mathrm{~d} y \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & -3 \log \left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)=-\log (\tan y)+\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \log \left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)=\log (\tan y)-\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)^3=\log \left(\frac{\tan y}{\mathrm{c}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1-\mathrm{e}^x\right)^3=\frac{\tan y}{\mathrm{c}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Put, $y=\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $x=1\,\,\,\,\,…[Given]$

We get

$$ \begin{aligned} & (1-e)^3=\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}}{c} \\ & \Rightarrow c=\frac{1}{(1-e)^3} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting value of 'c' in equation (i), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left(1-e^x\right)^3=\frac{\tan y}{\frac{1}{(1-e)^3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan y=\left(\frac{1-e^x}{1-e}\right)^3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the curve passing through the origin and satisfying the equation $\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 x y=4 x^2$, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=4 x^2\\ &\text { Dividing equation by }\left(1+x^2\right) \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{2 x y}{\left(1+x^2\right)}=\frac{4 x^2}{\left(1+x^2\right)} \\ & \text { I.F }=\mathrm{e}^{\text {Ipdx }}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{2 x}{1+x^2} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+x^2\right)} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I.F }=1+x^2 \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

Solution of differential equation is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & y \times I . F=\int Q \times I . F d x+c \\ & y\left(1+x^2\right)=\int\left(\frac{4 x^2}{1+x^2}\right)\left(1+x^2\right) d x+c \\ & y\left(1+x^2\right)=4 \int x^2 d x \\ & y\left(1+x^2\right)=4\left(\frac{x^3}{3}\right)+c \\ & 3 y\left(1+x^2\right)=4 x^3+3 c \\ & \operatorname{Put},(x, y)=(0,0) \\ & \Rightarrow 0=0+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad 3 y\left(1+x^2\right)=4 x^3$ is the required equation of the curve passing through origin.

content: The differential equation of all circles having their centres on the line $y=5$ and touching ( X -axis) is $\qquad$
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{h}, 5)$ be the centre of the circle on line $y=5$

Since the circle touches the X -axis

$$ \Rightarrow \text { radius }=5 $$

The equation of circle is

$$ (x-\mathrm{h})^2+(y-5)^2=5^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Differentiating w.r.t $(x)$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2(x-h)+2(y-5) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-h)=(5-y) \frac{d y}{d x} \end{aligned} $$

Equation (i) becomes

$$ \begin{aligned} & {\left[(5-y) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right]^2+(y-5)^2=5^2} \\ & (5-y)^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2+y^2-10 y=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: In a culture bacteria count is $1,00,000$ initially. The number increases by $10 \%$ in first 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach $2,00,000$, if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present?
explanation:
Let $x$ be the total number of bacteria present at time ( t )

According to given condition

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k} x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{x}=\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \log x=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Intitially, $x=1,00,000$

$\therefore \quad$ From (i)

$$ \log 1,00,000=c $$

Also, after 2 hours count increases by $10 \%$

$$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=2 \text { and } x=1,10,000 $$

From (i)

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log 1,10,000=2 k+\log 1,00,000 \\ & \Rightarrow k=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{11}{10}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Now, when $x=2,00,000$

then from (i)

$$ \log 2,00,000=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{11}{10}\right) t+\log 1,00,000 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{11}{10}\right) t=\log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow t=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \left(\frac{11}{10}\right)} \end{aligned} $$

content: A particular solution of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x+9 y)^2$, when $x=0, y=\frac{1}{27}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x+9 y)^2 \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{u}=x+9 y \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+9 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1+9 \mathrm{u}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+9 \mathrm{u}^2} \mathrm{du}=\mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\int \frac{1}{1+9 u^2} d u=\int d x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1}(3 \mathrm{u})=x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(3(x+9 y))=3 x+3 \mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(3 x+27 y)=3 x+3 \mathrm{c} \quad \ldots \text { (i) } \\ & \text { When } x=0, y=\frac{1}{27} \\ \therefore \quad & \tan ^{-1}(1)=0+3 \mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{c}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore \quad & \tan ^{-1}(3 x+27 y)=3 x+\frac{\pi}{4} \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x+27 y=\tan \left(3\left(x+\frac{\pi}{12}\right)\right) \end{array} $$

content: The general solution of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x y \mathrm{e}^{x^2}$ is

options.content: $y=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{e}^{x^2}} \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=\mathrm{e}^{-x^2} \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e}^2}} \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$y=\mathrm{e}^{x^2} \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x y \mathrm{e}^{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y=2 x \mathrm{e}^{x^2} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \int \frac{1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y=\int 2 x \mathrm{e}^{x^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } x^2=\mathrm{u} \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{du} \\ \therefore \quad & \log y=\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}} \mathrm{du} \\ \Rightarrow & \log y=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}}+\log \mathrm{c} \\ \Rightarrow & \log y-\log \mathrm{c}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}} \\ \Rightarrow & \log \left(\frac{y}{\mathrm{c}}\right)=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{y}{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}}} \\ \Rightarrow & y=\mathrm{c} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{u}} \\ \Rightarrow & y=\mathrm{c} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e}^{x^2}} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: Which of the following is not a homogeneous function?
explanation:
A function $f(x, y)$ is called homogeneous of degree $n$ if for all real numbers $k$,

$$ f(kx, ky) = k^n f(x, y) $$

Let's check each option:

Option A: $y^2 + 2xy$

Replace $x$ by $kx$ and $y$ by $ky$:

$ f(kx, ky) = (ky)^2 + 2(kx)(ky) = k^2 y^2 + 2 k^2 xy = k^2 (y^2 + 2xy) $

So, it's homogeneous of degree $2$.

Option B: $2x - 3y$

$ f(kx, ky) = 2(kx) - 3(ky) = k(2x - 3y) $

So, it's homogeneous of degree $1$.

Option C: $\sin\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$

$ f(kx, ky) = \sin\left(\frac{ky}{kx}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) $

So, $f(kx, ky) = f(x, y)$. This is homogeneous of degree $0$.

Option D: $\cos x + \sin y$

$ f(kx, ky) = \cos(kx) + \sin(ky) $

This is not a homogeneous function because it does not satisfy $f(kx, ky) = k^n f(x, y)$ for any $n$.

Final Answer:

Option D: $\boxed{\cos x+\sin y}$ is not a homogeneous function.

content: The assets of a person reduced in his business such that the rate of reduction is proportional to the square root of the existing assets. If the assets were initially ₹ 10 lakhs and due to loss they reduce to ₹ 10000 after 3 years, then the number of years required for the person to be bankrupt will be
explanation:
Let $x$ be the asset at time t .

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k} \sqrt{x}, \text { where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\sqrt{x}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 2 \sqrt{x}=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, x=10,00,000 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \sqrt{100000}=-\mathrm{k}(0)+\mathrm{c} \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{c}=2(1000)=2000 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \sqrt{x}=-\mathrm{kt}+2000 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=3, x=10,000 \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{10000}=-3 \mathrm{k}+2000 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(100)=-3 \mathrm{k}+2000 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{k}=1800 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=600 \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{x}=-600 \mathrm{t}+2000 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & \text { Time to go bankrupt }=\mathrm{T} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}, x=0 \\ \therefore & 0=-600 \mathrm{~T}+2000 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}=\frac{2000}{600}=\frac{10}{3} \text { years } \end{array} $$

content: If the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{x}{y}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{y}$ where a is constant, represents a family of circles then the radius of the circle is $\qquad$

options.content: $\mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2+2 \mathrm{c}}$, where c is the constant of integration
$\mathrm{a}^2+2 \mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$\sqrt{a+c}$, where $c$ is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{x}{y}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{y} \\ & \Rightarrow y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{a}-x \\ & \Rightarrow y \mathrm{~d} y=(\mathrm{a}-x) \mathrm{d} x \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int y \mathrm{~d} y=\int(\mathrm{a}-x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y^2}{2}=\mathrm{a} x-\frac{x^2}{2}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-2 \mathrm{a} x-2 \mathrm{c}=0 \end{aligned} $$

The above equation represents a circle with radius $(r)=\sqrt{a^2+2 c}$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $\cos \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=7, y=1$ at $x=0$ is
explanation: [Note: The equation $\cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)=7$ is invalid as $-1 \leq \cos \theta \leq 1$ for all real $\theta$.]

content: The solution of $\left(1+y^2\right)+\left(x-\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$ is

options.content: $2 x \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is the constant of integration
$x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is the constant of integration
$x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}=\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}+\mathrm{k}$, where k is the constant of integration
$\quad x=2+\mathrm{k} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\tan ^{-1} y}$, where k is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(1+y^2\right)+\left(x-\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}+\frac{x}{1+y^2}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^2} \mathrm{dy}}=\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Solution of the given equation is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^2} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y} \mathrm{~d} y+\mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \mathrm{tdt}+\mathrm{k} \quad \ldots\left[\text { Put } \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\mathrm{t}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}+\mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right)^2}{2}+\mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}+\mathrm{k} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The rate of reduction of a persons assets is proportional to the square root of the existing assets. The assets reduced from 25 lakhs to 6.25 lakhs in 2 years. This rate of reduction of his assets will make him bankrupt in
explanation:
Let $x$ represent the value of the asset at time $t$ years.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{x} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k} \sqrt{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\sqrt{x}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{array} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ 2 \sqrt{x}=-k t+c $$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, x=25$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \sqrt{25}=\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=10 \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{x}=-\mathrm{kt}+10 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=2, x=6.25 \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{6.25}=-2 \mathrm{k}+10\,\,\,\,\, \text { ...[From (i)] }\end{array} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \Rightarrow 2(2.5)=-2 \mathrm{k}+10 \\ & \Rightarrow 5=-2 \mathrm{k}+10 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{k}=5 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{5}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \sqrt{x}=\frac{-5}{2} \mathrm{t}+10 \,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots \text { [From (i)] }\\ & \text { Time to go bankrupt }=\mathrm{T} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}, x=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 0=\frac{-5}{2} \mathrm{~T}+10 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}=4 \text { years } \end{array} $$

content:
The general solution of

$x(x-1) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^3(2 x-1)+(x-2) y$ is

options.content: $y(x-1)=x^3+\mathrm{c}(x-1)$, where c is the constant of integration.
$y=x^3(x-1)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration.
$y(x-1)=x^3(x-1)+\mathrm{cx}^2$, where c is the constant of integration.
$y(x-1)=x^3(x-1)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration.

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x(x-1) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^3(2 x-1)+(x-2) y \\ \therefore \quad & x(x-1) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-(x-2) y=x^3(2 x-1) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-\frac{x-2}{x(x-1)} y=\frac{x^2(2 x-1)}{x-1} \end{array}\\ &\text { This is of the type }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{P} y=\mathrm{Q} \\ & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pd} x} \\ & \text { P }=\frac{2-x}{x(x-1)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{x-1} \\ \therefore \quad & 2-x=\mathrm{A}(x-1)+\mathrm{B} x \\ \therefore \quad & 2-x=(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) x-\mathrm{A} \\ \therefore \quad & A=-2 \text { and } \\ & A+B=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow B=1 \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pd} x} & =\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{2}{x} \mathrm{~d} x+\int \frac{1}{x-1} \mathrm{~d} x} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-2 \log x+\log (x-1)} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-\log x^2+\log (x-1)} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(\frac{x-1}{x^2}\right)} \\ & =\frac{x-1}{x^2} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Solution of the given equation is }\\ &\begin{aligned} y\left(\frac{x-1}{x^2}\right) & =\int \frac{x-1}{x^2} \cdot \frac{x^2(2 x-1)}{x-1} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & =x^2-x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore y(x-1) & =x^4-x^3+\mathrm{cx}^2 \\ \therefore y(x-1) & =x^3(x-1)+\mathrm{cx}^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The money invested in a company is compounded continuously. ₹ 400 invested today becomes ₹ 800 in 6 years, then at the end of 33 years, it will become .. $(\sqrt{2}=1.4142)$
explanation:
Here, Amount $(\mathrm{A})=Rs \, 800$

Principal $(\mathrm{P})=Rs\, 400, \mathrm{~N}=6$ years

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & A=P\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^N \\ \therefore & 800=400\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^6 \\ \therefore & 1+\frac{R}{100}=2^{\frac{1}{6}} \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & A=P\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^N \\ & =400\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^{33} \\ & =400\left(2^{\frac{1}{6}}\right)^{33} \\ & =400\left(2^5 2^{\frac{1}{2}}\right) \\ & =400 \times 32 \times 1.4142 \\ & =Rs\,\, 18101.76 \end{aligned} $$

content: The sum of the degree and order of the differential equation $\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}}=\sqrt[5]{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-5}$ is
explanation:
$$ \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}}=\sqrt[5]{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-5} $$

Taking tenth power of both sides, we get

$$ \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)^5=\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-5\right)^2 $$

$\therefore \quad$ By definition,

order $=2$ and degree $=5$

$\therefore \quad$ Order + degree $=2+5=7$

content: The differential equation whose solution represents the family $x^2 y=4 \mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is an arbitrary constant, is
explanation:
Given the family of solutions:

$$ x^2 y = 4 e^x + c $$

Let's find the differential equation:

Step 1: Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$

Using the product rule on the left,

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 y) = \frac{d}{dx}(4 e^x + c) $$

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 y) = x^2 \frac{dy}{dx} + 2x y $$

$$ \frac{d}{dx}(4 e^x + c) = 4 e^x $$

So,

$$ x^2 \frac{dy}{dx} + 2x y = 4 e^x $$

Step 2: Rearranging terms

Bring all terms to one side:

$$ x^2 \frac{dy}{dx} + 2x y - 4e^x = 0 $$

This is the required differential equation.

Matching with the given options:

  • Option D: $x^2 \frac{dy}{dx} + 2x y - 4e^x = 0$

Final Answer:

Option D is correct.

content: The differential equation of all straight lines passing through the point $(1,-1)$ is
explanation:
The equation of the family of lines passing through $(1,-1)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y+1=m(x-1) \\ & \Rightarrow y=m(x-1)-1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=m \end{aligned} $$

Substituting the value of m in (i), we get

$$ y=\frac{d y}{d x}(x-1)-1 $$

content:
The principal increases continuously in a newly opened bank at the rate of $10 \%$ per year. An amount of Rs. 2000 is deposited with this bank. How much will it become after 5 years?

$$ \left(\mathrm{e}^{0.5}=1.648\right) $$
explanation:
Here, $\mathrm{A}=$ final amount after time t

Principal $(\mathrm{P})=Rs \, 2000$, annual rate $(\mathrm{r})=10 \%$ time $(\mathrm{t})=5$ years.

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{A} & =\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{rt}} \\ & =2000 \times \mathrm{e}^{0.10 \times 5} \\ & =2000 \times \mathrm{e}^{0.5} \\ & =2000 \times 1.648 \\ & =3296 \end{aligned} $$

content: The solution of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x+y)^2$ is

options.content: $\tan ^{-1}(x+y)=x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$x+y=\tan x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$x+y=\cot ^{-1} x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration
$x+y=\sin ^{-1}(x+y)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is the constant of integration

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x+y)^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Put } x+y=\mathrm{v} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}-1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}-1=\mathrm{v}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}^2+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{v}^2+1}=\mathrm{d} x \end{aligned} $$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} v=x+c \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(x+y)=x+c \end{aligned} $$

content: A normal is drawn at a point $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$ of a curve $y=\mathrm{f}(x)$. The normal meets the $X$ axis at $Q$. $l(\mathrm{PQ})=\mathrm{k} \cdot(\mathrm{k}$ is a constant) Then equation of the curve through $(0, k)$ is
explanation:
We are finding the equation of a curve $y = f(x)$ such that, if we draw a normal (a line at right angles to the curve) at any point $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$, it meets the $X$-axis at a point $\mathrm{Q}$, and the length $\mathrm{PQ}$ is always equal to $\mathrm{k}$ (a fixed constant). The curve passes through $(0,\ k)$.

Step 1: Equation of the Normal

The slope of the curve at point $(x, y)$ is $\frac{dy}{dx} = m$. So, the slope of the normal (which is at a right angle to the tangent) is $-\frac{1}{m}$.

The equation of the normal at point $P(x, y)$ is:
$\mathrm{Y} - y = \frac{-1}{m}(\mathrm{X} - x)$

Step 2: Where the Normal Meets the $X$-axis

To find where this normal meets the $X$-axis, set $\mathrm{Y} = 0$ and solve for $\mathrm{X}$.
$\Rightarrow \; 0 - y = \frac{-1}{m}(\mathrm{X} - x)$
$\Rightarrow \; \mathrm{X} = x + y m$
So, the coordinates of $Q$ are $\left(x + y \frac{dy}{dx},\ 0\right)$.

Step 3: Finding the Length $\mathrm{PQ}$

The distance from $P(x, y)$ to $Q(x + y \frac{dy}{dx},\ 0)$ is:
$\mathrm{PQ} = \sqrt{\left( y \frac{dy}{dx} \right)^2 + y^2}$

Since this must be equal to the constant $k$, we have:
$\sqrt{\left( y \frac{dy}{dx} \right)^2 + y^2} = k$

Step 4: Rearranging the Equation

Square both sides:
$\left( y \frac{dy}{dx} \right)^2 + y^2 = k^2$

Subtract $y^2$ from both sides:
$\left( y \frac{dy}{dx} \right)^2 = k^2 - y^2$

Take the square root of both sides:
$y \frac{dy}{dx} = \pm \sqrt{k^2 - y^2}$

Step 5: Separate the Variables

Write it as:
$\frac{y\ dy}{\sqrt{k^2 - y^2}} = \pm dx$

Step 6: Integrate Both Sides

Integrate both sides to find the relation between $x$ and $y$:
$\int \frac{y\ dy}{\sqrt{k^2 - y^2}} = \pm \int dx$
The integral on the left side is $-\sqrt{k^2 - y^2}$, so:
$-\sqrt{k^2 - y^2} = \pm x + c$, where $c$ is a constant.

Step 7: Use the Given Point

The curve passes through $(0, k)$, so plug in $x = 0$ and $y = k$:
$-\sqrt{k^2 - k^2} = \pm 0 + c$
$-\sqrt{0} = c \implies c = 0$

Step 8: Final Equation

So, the equation is:
$-\sqrt{k^2 - y^2} = \pm x$
Square both sides:
$x^2 + y^2 = k^2$

content: The order and degree of differential equation of all tangent lines to the parabola $x^2=4 y$ is respectively.
explanation:
Given equation of parabola

$$x^2=4 y$$

The equation of tangent to parabola $x^2=4 y$ is

$$x=\mathrm{m} y+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~m}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{m} y+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}} \quad \ldots[\because a=1]\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m} x=\mathrm{m}^2 y+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} x=\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2 y+1 \end{aligned}$$

The order and degree of above differential equation is 1 and 2.

content: If $y=y(x)$ satisfies $\left(\frac{2+\sin x}{1+y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\cos x$ such that $y(0)=2$, then $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{2+\sin x}{1+y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{1+y}=\left(\frac{-\cos x}{2+\sin x}\right) d x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{d y}{1+y}=-\int \frac{\cos x}{2+\sin x} d x \\ & \Rightarrow \log (1+y)+\log (2+\sin x)=\log c \\ & \Rightarrow(1+y)(2+\sin x)=c\quad\text{...... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since $y(0)=2$, i.e. $y=2$ when $x=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad(1+2)(2+\sin 0)=\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=6 \\ & \therefore \quad (y+1)(2+\sin x)=6 \quad \ldots[\text { From (i) }]\\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow y & =\frac{6}{2+\sin x}-1 \\ \therefore \quad y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) & =\frac{6}{2+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}}-1 \\ & =\frac{6}{2+1}-1 \\ & =2-1 \\ & =1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: In a bank, the principal increases continuously at a rate of $x \%$ per year. Then the rate $x$, if ₹$100$ double itself in 10 years, is ( $\log 2=0.6931$)
explanation:
Principal increases at a rate of $x \%$ per year

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{x}{100} \times \mathrm{P} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{x}{100} \mathrm{dt} \end{array}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\log \mathrm{P}=\frac{x}{100} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}\quad\text{...... (i)}$$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{P}=100$

$$\Rightarrow \log 100=\mathrm{c}$$

$\Rightarrow$ Also, when $\mathrm{t}=10, \mathrm{P}=200$

From (i)

$$\begin{aligned} & \log 200=\frac{x}{100} \times 10+\log 100 \\ & \Rightarrow \log 200-\log 100=\frac{x}{10} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{10}=\log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=10 \log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=6.931 \% \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=1$ at $x=y=1$ with $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$ at $x=1$, is
explanation:
Given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{1}{x} \end{aligned}$$

Integration on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}=\log x+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{..... (i)}\\ & \text { Given } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \text { at } x=1 \\ \therefore \quad & 0=\log 1+\mathrm{c} \quad \text{....[From (i)]}\\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{c}=0\quad\text{..... (ii)} \end{array}$$

Again integrating (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y=x \log x-x+c x+k \\ & y=x \log x-x+k \quad \text{...(iii)[From (ii)]} \end{aligned}$$

At $x=y=1$

$$\begin{aligned} & 1=(1) \log (1)-1+\mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ From (iii)

$$y=x \log x-x+2.$$

content: The slope of tangent at $(x, y)$ to a curve passing through $\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is $\frac{y}{x}-\cos ^2 \frac{y}{x}$, then the equation of curve is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y}{x}-\cos ^2 \frac{y}{x} \quad\text{..... (i)}\\ & \text { Put } y=\mathrm{v} x \quad\qquad\text{..... (ii)}\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\quad\text{..... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}-\cos ^2 \mathrm{v} \quad \Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-\cos ^2 \mathrm{v}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \sec ^2 v d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}+c \\ & \Rightarrow \tan v=-\log x+c \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \frac{y}{x}=-\log x+c\quad\text{.... (iv)} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since the required curve passes through }\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=-\log 1+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \tan \frac{y}{x}=-\log x+1 \quad\ldots[\text { From (iv) }]\\ & \Rightarrow \tan \frac{y}{x}=-\log x+\log \mathrm{e} \\ & \Rightarrow y=x \tan ^{-1}\left[\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{e}}{x}\right)\right] \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $y(x)$ represented by $x=\sin t$, $y=a e^{t \sqrt{2}}+b e^{t \sqrt{2}}, t \in\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ satisfies the equation $\left(1-x^2\right) y^{\prime \prime}-x y^{\prime}=\mathrm{k} y$, then the value of k is k is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} x & =\sin t \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\cos \mathrm{t} \\ y & =a e^{\mathrm{t} \sqrt{2}}+b e^{t \sqrt{2}} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{t} \sqrt{2}}(a+b) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{d y}{d t}=(a+b) \cdot e^{t \sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \\ \therefore & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}}{\cos t} \\ \therefore & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}}{\cos t}\right) \cdot \frac{d t}{d x} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{2}(a+b)\left[\frac{\cos t\left(e^{t \sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2}\right)-e^{i \sqrt{2}}(-\sin t)}{(\cos t)^2}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\cos t} \\ & =\sqrt{2}(a+b)\left[\frac{e^{i \sqrt{2}}(\sqrt{2} \cos t+\sin t)}{\cos ^3 t}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(1-x^2\right) y^{\prime \prime}-x y^{\prime} \\ & =\left(1-\sin ^2 t\right) \sqrt{2}(a+b)\left[\frac{e^{t \sqrt{2}}(\sqrt{2} \cos t+\sin t)}{\cos ^3 t}\right] -\sin t\left[\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}}{\cos t}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$\begin{aligned}=\left(\cos ^2 t\right) \sqrt{2}(a+b)\left[\frac{e^{t \sqrt{2}}(\sqrt{2} \cos t+\sin t)}{\cos ^3 t}\right] -\sin t\left[\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}}{\cos t}\right]\end{aligned}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}(\sqrt{2} \cos t+\sin t-\sin t)}{\cos t} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{2}(a+b) e^{t \sqrt{2}}(\sqrt{2} \cos t)}{\cos t} \\ & =2\left(a e^{t \sqrt{2}}+b e^{t \sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & =2 y \\ & \therefore \quad k=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The rate of growth of bacteria in a culture is proportional to the number of bacteria present and the bacteria count is 1000 at $\mathrm{t}=0$. The number of bacteria is increased by $20 \%$ in 2 hours. If the population of bacteria is 2000 after $\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right)}$ hours, then $\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log 2}\right)^2$ is
explanation:
Let ' $x$ ' be the number of bacteria present at time ' $t$ '.

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto x$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\lambda x$, where $\lambda$ is the constant of proportionality

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \log x=\lambda t+c \\ & \text { When } t=0, x=1000 \\ \therefore \quad & \log 1000=0+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=\log (1000) \\ \therefore \quad & \log x=\lambda t+\log (1000)\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { When } t=2 \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} x & =1000+(20 \% \text { of } 1000) \\ & =1000+200 \\ & =1200 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log 1200=2 \lambda+\log 1000 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{1200}{1000}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \log x=\frac{t}{2} \log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right)+\log (1000)\quad\text{.... [From (i)]} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right)}, x=2000 \\ & \therefore \quad \log 2000=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right)} \times \frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{6}{5}\right)+\log (1000) \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{2000}{1000}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2} \Rightarrow \log 2=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log 2}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\log 2}\right)^2=2^2=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y \tan x-y^2 \sec x$ is

options.content: $\tan x=(\mathrm{c}+\sec x) y$, where c is constant of integration.
$\sec y=(\mathrm{c}+\tan y) x$, where c is constant of integration.
$\sec x=(\mathrm{c}+\tan x) y$, where c is constant of integration.
$\cos y=(\mathrm{c}+\tan y)$, where c is constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y \tan x-y^2 \sec x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\tan x\left(-\frac{1}{y}\right)=-\sec x \ldots \end{aligned}$$

Put $\mathrm{v}=-\frac{1}{y} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\frac{1}{y^2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}+(\tan x) \mathrm{v}=-\sec x \quad\text{...[From (i)]}$

$\therefore \quad$ I.F. $=\mathrm{e}^{\int \tan x \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log (\sec x)}=\sec x$

$\therefore \quad$ solution of the given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { v. } \sec x=\int-\sec x \cdot \sec x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v} \sec x=-\tan x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{1}{y} \sec x=-\tan x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \sec x=y(\tan x+\mathrm{c}), \text { where } \mathrm{c}=-\mathrm{c}_1 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $(x \log x) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 x \log x(x \geq 1)$ then $y(\mathrm{e})$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given, }(x \log x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=2 x \log x \\ & \text { When } x=1, y=0 \\ & (x \log x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=2 x \log x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=2 \\ & \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{x \log x} \mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log (\log x)}=\log x \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Solution of the given equation is } \\ & y \cdot \log x=\int 2 \log x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & y \log x=2(x \log x-x)+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { When } x=1, y=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 0=-2+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2 \\ \therefore \quad & y \log x=2(x \log x-x)+2 \\ \therefore \quad & y(\mathrm{e})=2(\mathrm{e}-\mathrm{e})+2=2 \end{array}$$

content: If $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+3, y+3>0$ and $y(0)=2$, then $y(\log 2)$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{y+3}=\mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \int \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y+3}=\int \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y+3)=x+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & y=2 \text { when } x=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \log (2+3)=0+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 5 \\ \therefore \quad & \log (y+3)=x+\log 5 \quad\text{.... [From (i)]}\\ & \Rightarrow y+3=5 \mathrm{e}^x \\ & \Rightarrow y=5 \mathrm{e}^x-3 \\ \therefore \quad & y(\log 2)=5 \mathrm{e}^{\log 2}-3=10-3=7 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The assets of a person are reduced in his business such that the rate of reduction is proportional to the square root of the existing assets. If the assets were initially ₹$10,00,000$ and due to loss they reduce to ₹$ 10,000$ after 3 years, then the number of years required for the person to go bankrupt will be
explanation:
Let $x$ be the asset at time t .
