$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k} \sqrt{x}, \text { where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\sqrt{x}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$2 \sqrt{x}=-k t+c$$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, x=10,00,000$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \sqrt{1000000}=-k(0)+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=2(1000)=2000 \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{x}=-k t+2000\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=3, x=10,000 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \sqrt{10000}=-3 \mathrm{k}+2000 \\ \Rightarrow & 2(100)=-3 \mathrm{k}+2000 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 \mathrm{k}=1800 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=600 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \sqrt{x}=-600 \mathrm{t}+2000\quad\text{.... [From (i)]} \end{array}\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Time to go bankrupt }=\mathrm{T} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}, x=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 0=-600 \mathrm{~T}+2000 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{~T}=\frac{2000}{600}=\frac{10}{3} \text { years } \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The order of the differential equation, whose solution is $y=\left(C_1+C_2\right) \mathrm{e}^x+C_3 \mathrm{e}^{x+C_4}$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} y & =\left(\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2\right) \mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{e}^{x+\mathrm{C}_4} \\ & =\mathrm{Ae}^x+\mathrm{Be}^x \text {, where } \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2 \text { and } \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{e}_4 \\ & =(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) \mathrm{e}^x \\ \end{aligned} \\ & =\mathrm{ De^x}, \text {where } \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { This equation consist of one arbitrary constant. } \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 e^{2 x}+3 e^{4 x}}{e^x+e^{-x}}$ is

options.content: $y=\mathrm{e}^{-3 x}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=\mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=\mathrm{e}^{3 x}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=\mathrm{e}^{-x}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 \mathrm{e}^{2 x}+3 \mathrm{e}^{4 x}}{\mathrm{e}^x+\mathrm{e}^{-x}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 \mathrm{e}^{2 x}\left(1+\mathrm{e}^{2 x}\right)}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^{2 x}+1}{\mathrm{e}^x}\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3 \mathrm{e}^{2 x} \cdot \mathrm{e}^x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} y=3 \mathrm{e}^{3 x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &y=\mathrm{e}^{3 x}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation, having general solution as $A x^2+B y^2=1$, where $A$ and $B$ are arbitrary constants, is

options.content: $x y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-x\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2-y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$
$x y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-x\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$
$x y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+x\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$
$x y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+x\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2-y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$

explanation:
$$\mathrm{A} x^2+\mathrm{By} y^2=1$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$2 \mathrm{~A} x+2 \mathrm{~B} y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Again, differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$2 \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{~B}\left[\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2+y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dx}}{ }^2\right]=0\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Solving (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &x y \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}+x\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2-y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: A radio active substance has half-life of $h$ days, then its initial decay rate is given by Note that at $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{o}}$
explanation:
Let $M$ be the mass of substance at time $t$. Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dM}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{kM} \text {, where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dM}}{\mathrm{M}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\log M=-k t+c$$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{m}_0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log m_0=0+c \\ \therefore & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log m_0 \\ & \log M=-k t+\log m_0 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{M}{m_0}=-k t \end{array}$$

When $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{M}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_0$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_0}{m_0}\right)=-k h \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{1}{2}=-k h \\ & \Rightarrow \log 2=k h \\ & \Rightarrow k=\frac{\log 2}{h}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Initial decay rate,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{dM}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-\mathrm{km}_0 \\ & =\frac{-\mathrm{m}_0}{\mathrm{~h}} \log 2\quad\text{...[From (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of $y=\mathrm{e}^x\left(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{bx}+x^2\right)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \begin{array}{l} y=\mathrm{e}^x\left(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x+x^2\right) \\ \therefore\quad\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^x\left(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} x+x^2\right)+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b}+2 x) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b}+2 x) \quad\text{... (i)}\\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b}+2 x)+\mathrm{e}^x(2) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y+2 \mathrm{e}^x \quad\text{... [From (i)]}\\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-2 \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-2 \mathrm{e}^x+y=0 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: An ice ball melts at the rate which is proportional to the amount of ice at that instant. Half of the quantity of ice melts in 15 minutes. $x_0$ is the initial quantity of ice. If after 30 minutes the amount of ice left is $\mathrm{kx}_0$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
Given that half of the ice melts in 15 minutes.

$\therefore \quad$ In 30 minutes, $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{\mathrm{h}}$ of the ice will melt.

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{4}$

content: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=x \log x,(x>1)$ If $2(y(2))=\log 4-1$ then the value of $y(\mathrm{e})$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=x \log x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{1}{x} y=\log x \\ & \text { Here, } \mathrm{P}(x)=\frac{1}{x}, \mathrm{Q}(x)=\log x \\ \therefore & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{P}(x) \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log x}=x \\ \therefore \quad & y(\text { I.F. })=\int \mathrm{Q}(\text { I.F. }) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & x y=\int x \log x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & x y=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\left(\int \frac{x^2}{2} \times \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & x y=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\frac{1}{2} \int x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore & x y=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\frac{1}{4} x^2+\mathrm{c}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}\\ &\text { Given that } 2(y(2))=\log 4-1\\ &\therefore \quad y(2)=\log 2-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \text { From equation (i), we get } \\ & 2\left(\log 2-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{(2)^2}{2} \log 2-\frac{1}{4}(2)^2+c \\ \therefore & 2 \log 2-1=2 \log 2-1+c \\ \therefore \quad & c=0\quad\text{... (ii)} \end{array}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { From (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &y(\mathrm{e})=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{e}}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y(x)$ is the solution of the differential equation $(x+2) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^2+4 x-9, x \neq-2$ and $y(0)=0$, then $y(-4)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & (x+2) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^2+4 x-9 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~d} y=\frac{\left(x^2+4 x+4\right)-4-9}{x+2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~d} y=\frac{(x+2)^2-13}{(x+2)} \mathrm{d} x \end{array}$$

Integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \mathrm{d} y=\int(x+2) \mathrm{d} x-13 \int \frac{1}{x+2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & y=\frac{(x+2)^2}{2}-13 \log |x+2|+c\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Given that $y(0)=0$

$\therefore \quad$ from equation (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \\ \therefore \quad & =2-13 \log |0+2|+c \\ & \quad c=13 \log (2)-2\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ from (i) and (ii), we get

$$y(-4)=2-13 \log (2)+13 \log (2)-2=0$$

content: The bacteria increase at the rate proportional to the number of bacteria present. If the original number N doubles in 8 hours, then the number of bacteria in 24 hours will be
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Initial number of bacteria }=\mathrm{N} \\ \therefore & \text { After } 8 \text { hours number of bacteria }=2 \mathrm{~N} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { After } 16 \text { hours number of bacteria }=4 \mathrm{~N} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { After } 24 \text { hours number of bacteria }=8 \mathrm{~N} \end{array}$$

content: The general solution of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\sin \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)-\sin \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-2 \sin \left(\frac{y}{2}\right) \cdot \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int \operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) d y=-\int 2 \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) d x+c_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\log \tan \left(\frac{y}{4}\right)}{\frac{1}{2}}=-\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)}{\frac{1}{2}}+c_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \tan \left(\frac{y}{4}\right)=C-2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right), \text { where } C=\frac{1}{2} c_1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation, $x y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^2+2 y^2$ when $y(1)=0$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=x^2+2 y^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x^2+2 y^2}{x y}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Put $y=v x\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\quad\text{.... (iii)}$$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x^2+2 \mathrm{v}^2 x^2}{x(\mathrm{vx})} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x^2\left(1+2 \mathrm{v}^2\right)}{x^2 \mathrm{v}} \\ \therefore \quad & x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1+2 \mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{v}} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{v}}{1+\mathrm{v}^2} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Integrating on both sides, we get } \\ & \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^2} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+v^2\right|=\log |x|+\log \left|c_1\right| \end{aligned}$$

$\begin{aligned} \log \left|1+v^2\right| & =2 \log |x|+2 \log \left|c_1\right| \\ & =\log \left|x^2\right|+\log |c| \ldots\left[\log c_1^2=\log c\right]\end{aligned}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log \left|1+v^2\right|=\log \left|c x^2\right| \\ \therefore & 1+v^2=c x^2 \\ \therefore & 1+\frac{y^2}{x^2}=c x^2 \\ \therefore & x^2+y^2=c x^4\quad\text{.... (iv)} \end{array}$$

Putting $x=1$ and $y=0$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 1+0=c(1) \\ \therefore\quad& c=1 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\mathrm{c}=1 \mathrm{in}$ (iv), we get

$$x^2+y^2=x^4$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\mathrm{e}^{y-x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{1+y \log y}\right)$ is

options.content: $\mathrm{e}^y \log y=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sin x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\mathrm{e}^y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\log y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y \log y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{e}^{y-x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{1+y \log y}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{e}^y}{\mathrm{e}^x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y}{(1+y \log y)}(\sin x+\cos x) \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{e}^y \frac{(1+y \log y)}{y} \mathrm{~d} y=\mathrm{e}^x(\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{e}^y\left(\log y+\frac{1}{y}\right) \mathrm{d} y=\mathrm{e}^x(\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x \end{array}\\ &\text { Integrating both sides, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{e}^y \log y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \quad \ldots\left[\because \int \mathrm{e}^x\left[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)\right] \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{c}\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: A spherical rain drop evaporates at a rate proportional to its surface area. If initially its radius is 3 mm and after 1 second it is reduced to 2 mm , then at any time t its radius is (where $0 \leq \mathrm{t}<3$)
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto-\mathrm{s} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{ks}, \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \mathrm{v}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \text { and } \mathrm{s}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Equation (i) becomes }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{r}=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{r}=3 \\ \therefore \quad & 3=-\mathrm{k}(0)+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=3 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{r}=-\mathrm{kt}+3 \quad\text{...[From (ii)]}\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=1, \mathrm{r}=2 \\ \therefore \quad & 2=-\mathrm{k}(1)+3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{r}=-\mathrm{t}+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=3-\mathrm{t} \end{array}$$

content:
The order of the differential equation, whose general solution is given by

$$y=\left(c_1+c_2\right) \cos \left(x+c_3\right)-c_4 e^{x+c 5}$$

where $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$ and $c_5$ are arbitrary constant, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\left(c_1+c_2\right) \cos \left(x+c_3\right)-c_4 e^{x+c^5} \\ &=\left(c_1+c_2\right) \cos \left(x+c_3\right)-c_4 e^x e^{c_5} \\ &=A \cos \left(x+c_3\right)-B e^x \\ & \quad \ldots\left[A=c_1+c_2, B=c_4 e^{c_5}\right] \end{aligned}$$

There are 3 arbitrary constants.

$\therefore\quad$ Order of the given differential equation is 3.

content: If $\cos x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y \sin x=6 x, 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$, then general solution of the differential equation is

options.content: $y=\cos x+3 x^2+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y+\cos x=3 x^2+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=3 x^2 \cos x+\cos x$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y \cdot \cos x=3 x^2+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \cos x \frac{d y}{d x}-y \sin x=6 x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-(\tan x) y=6 x \sec x \end{aligned}\\ &\text { This equation is of the form } \frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ If $=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{p} d x}=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \tan x d x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log x \cos x}=\cos x$

$\therefore \quad$ Solution of given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & y \cdot \cos x=\int 6 x \times \sec x \times \cos x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y \cos x=\int 6 x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y \cos x=3 x^2+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1}$ is

options.content: $y=x+\log (x+y)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=x-\log (x+y)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=x-\log (2 x+y)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y=x^2+\log (x+y)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1} \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Put } x+y=v \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}-1\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}-1=\frac{\mathrm{v}+1}{\mathrm{v}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}-1} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{v}-1}{2 \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}=\mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \log \mathrm{v}=x+\mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}-\log \mathrm{v}=2 x+2 \mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-\log (x+y)=2 x+2 \mathrm{c}_1 \\ & \Rightarrow y=x+\log (x+y)+\mathrm{c}, \text { where } \mathrm{c}=2 \mathrm{c}_1 \end{aligned}$$

content: A radio-active substance has a half-life of h days, then its initial decay rate is given by (where radio-active substance has initial mass $\mathrm{m}_0$)
explanation:
Let $m$ be the mass of substance at time $t$. Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{km} \text {, where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{~m}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

Integrating on both sides, we get $\log \mathrm{m}=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}$

When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~m}=\mathrm{m}_0$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log m_0=0+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=\log m_0 \\ \therefore \quad & \log m=-k t+\log m_0 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{m}{m_0}=-k t \end{array}$$

When $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_0$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_0}{m_0}\right)=-\mathrm{kh} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{1}{2}=-\mathrm{kh} \\ & \Rightarrow \log 2=\mathrm{kh} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{\log 2}{\mathrm{~h}}\quad\text{.... (i)}\\ &\text { Initial decay rate, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-\mathrm{km}_0 \\ & =\frac{-\mathrm{m}_0}{\mathrm{~h}} \log 2\quad\text{.... [From (i)]} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^2-y^2}}{x}$ is

options.content: $\sin ^{-1} y=\log x+c$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\frac{y}{x}=\sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\frac{y}{x}=\sqrt{x^2-y^2}+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^2-y^2}}{x}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Put $y=v x\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{d} x}\quad\text{.... (iii)}$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v} x+\sqrt{x^2-\mathrm{v}^2 x^2}}{x} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}+\sqrt{1-\mathrm{v}^2} \\ \therefore & x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{v}^2} \\ \therefore & \int \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{v}^2}}=\int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x} \\ \therefore & \sin ^{-1}(\mathrm{v})=\log |x|+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore & \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|+\mathrm{c} \end{array}$$

content: In a certain culture of bacteria, the rate of increase is proportional to the number present. If there are $10^4$ at the end of 3 hours and $4 \cdot 10^4$ at the end of 5 hours, then there were _________ the beginning.
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } x \text { be the number of bacteria present at time } t \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto x \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kx} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{x}=\mathrm{kdt} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$\log x=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

When $\mathrm{t}=3, x=10^4=10,000$

Equation (i) becomes

$\log (10,000)=3 \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{c}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

When $\mathrm{t}=5, x=4.10^4=40,000$

Equation (i) becomes

$\log (40,000)=5 k+c\quad\text{.... (iii)}$

Subtracting (ii) from (iii), we get

$\mathrm{k}=\log 2$

From equation (ii),

$$\log (10,000)=3 \log 2+c$$

$$\therefore \quad c=\log \left(\frac{10^4}{8}\right)$$

Now, Initially $\mathrm{t}=0$

From (i),

$$\begin{aligned} & \log x=\mathrm{k} \times 0+\log \left(\frac{10^4}{8}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \log x=\log \left(\frac{10^4}{8}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & x=\frac{10^4}{8} \end{aligned}$$

content: Integrating factor of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{y}{x} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x} \\ \therefore & \quad \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right) \mathrm{d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{x-\log x}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^x}{x} \end{aligned}$$

content: The population of a town increases at a rate proportional to the population at that time. If the population increases from 40 thousand to 80 thousand in 40 years, then the population in another 40 years will be
explanation:
Let p be the population at time $t$ years.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Then } \frac{d p}{d t}=k p \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d p}{p}=k d t \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$\log \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}$

when $t=0, p=40000$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \log 40000=0+c \\ & \quad \Rightarrow c=\log 40000 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \log \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{kt}+\log 40000 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{p}}{40000}\right)=\mathrm{kt}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

When $\mathrm{t}=40$ years, $\mathrm{p}=80000$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{80000}{40000}\right)=40 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{40} \log 2 \\ \therefore \quad & \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{p}}{40000}\right)=\frac{1}{40} \log 2 \times \mathrm{t}\quad\text{.... [From (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Population after another 40 years, i.e., $\mathrm{t}=80$ years, we have

$$\begin{aligned} & \log \left(\frac{p}{40000}\right)=\frac{1}{40} \log 2 \times 80 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{p}{40000}=2 \log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{p}{40000}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow p=16,0000 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=y(x)$ is the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}+2 y=x^2$ satisfying $y(1)=1$, then the value of $y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^2 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2}{x}\right) y=x \\ & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2}{x} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \log x}=x^2 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Solution of differential equation is } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & y \cdot x^2=\int x \cdot x^2 \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & y x^2=\frac{x^4}{4}+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & y=\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{x^2}\quad\text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Given, $y(1)=1$

$\Rightarrow 1=\frac{1}{4}+\mathrm{c}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{3}{4}$

$\therefore \quad$ equation (i) becomes,

$$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{3}{4 x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & y\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}{4}+\frac{3}{4 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}=\frac{1}{16}+3=\frac{49}{16} \end{aligned}$$

content: The curve satisfying the differential equation $y \mathrm{~d} x-\left(x+3 y^2\right) \mathrm{dy}=0$ and passing through the point $(1,1)$ also passes through the point
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y \mathrm{~d} x-\left(x+3 y^2\right) \mathrm{d} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y \mathrm{~d} x=\left(x+3 y^2\right) \mathrm{d} y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{x+3 y^2}{y} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{x}{y}+3 y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}-\left(\frac{1}{y}\right) x=3 y \end{aligned}$$

Which is a linear equation

$$\therefore\quad\mathrm{IF}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{-1}{y} d y}=\mathrm{e}^{-\log y}=\frac{1}{y}$$

$\therefore\quad$ The required solution is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \frac{1}{y}=\int 3 y \times \frac{1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore\quad & \frac{x}{y}=3 y+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow x=3 y^2+\mathrm{cy}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Curve passes through $(1,1)$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1=3+c \\ & \Rightarrow c=-2 \end{aligned}$$

Equation (i) becomes,

$$x=3 y^2-2 y$$

Option (D) i.e., $\left(\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right)$

Satisfies above equation.

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{1}{x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\tan ^{-1}$ is

options.content: $y+\frac{x^2 \tan ^{-1} x}{2}+\mathrm{c}=0$, where c is a constant of integration.
$y+x \tan ^{-1} x+\mathrm{c}=0$, where c is a constant integration.
$y-x-\tan ^{-1} x+\mathrm{c}=0$, where is a constant of integration.
$y=\frac{x^2 \tan ^{-1} x}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\tan ^{-1} x\right)+\mathrm{c}$, where c is constant of integration.

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\tan ^{-1} x \\ & \mathrm{~d} y=x \cdot \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Integrating on both sides we get, } \\ & y=\int x \cdot \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & y=\tan ^{-1} x \int x \mathrm{~d} x-\int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \tan ^{-1} x \cdot \int x \mathrm{~d} x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & y=\tan ^{-1} x \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}-\int\left(\frac{1}{1+x^2} \times \frac{x^2}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & y=\tan ^{-1} x \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\frac{x^2}{1+x^2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{x^2 \cdot \tan ^{-1} x}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\int \frac{x^2+1}{x^2+1}-\int \frac{1}{1+x^2} \mathrm{~d} x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{x^2 \tan ^{-1} x}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\tan ^{-1} x\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation obtained by eliminating arbitrary constant from the equation $y^2=(x+c)^3$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &y^2=(x+c)^3\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.to } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=3(x+\mathrm{c})^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+\mathrm{c})^2=\frac{2 y}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \Rightarrow(x+\mathrm{c})^6=\left(\frac{2 y}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left((x+\mathrm{c})^3\right)^2=\frac{8 y^3}{27}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(y^2\right)^2=\frac{8 y^3}{27}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow y^4=\frac{8 y^3}{27}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 27 y=8\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The decay rate of radium is proportional to the amount present at any time $t$. If initially 60 gms was present and half life period of radium is 1600 years, then the amount of radium present after 3200 years is
explanation:
Let $m$ be the mass of substance at time $t$.

Then

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{km}, \text { where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{~m}}=-\mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\log m=-k t+c$$

when $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~m}=60 \mathrm{~gms}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log 60=-k(0)+c \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 60 \\ \therefore \quad & \log \mathrm{~m}=-\mathrm{kt}+\log 60 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad &\text { when } \mathrm{t}=1600, \mathrm{~m}=\frac{60}{2}=30 \mathrm{gms}\\ \therefore \quad & \log 30=-1600 \mathrm{k}+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow 1600 \mathrm{k}=\log 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{1600} \log 2 \end{array}$$

Equation (i) becomes

$$\log m=\frac{-1}{1600}(\log 2) t+\log 60$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { When } t=3200 \text { years }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \log \mathrm{m}=\frac{-1}{1600} \times \log 2 \times 3200+\log 60 \\ & \log \mathrm{~m}=-\log 4+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \mathrm{~m}=\log \frac{60}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=15 \text { grams } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of differential equation $\left(1+y^2\right)(1+\log x) \mathrm{d} x+x \mathrm{~d} y=0$ at $x=1, y=1$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \left(1+y^2\right)(1+\log x) \mathrm{d} x+x \mathrm{~d} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(1+y^2\right)(1+\log x) \mathrm{d} x=-x \mathrm{~d} y \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1+\log x}{x}\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left(\frac{-1}{1+y^2}\right) \mathrm{d} y \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Integrating on both sides, we get }\\ &\Rightarrow \int \frac{(1+\log x)}{x} \mathrm{~d} x=-1 \int \frac{1}{1+y^2} \mathrm{~d} y \end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{tdt}=-\tan ^{-1} y+\mathrm{c} \quad \ldots\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { Let } 1+\log x=\mathrm{t} \\ \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}\end{array}\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}=-\tan ^{-1} y+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(1+\log x)^2}{2}=-\tan ^{-1} y+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { At } x=1, y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(1+\log 1)^2}{2}=-\tan ^{-1}(1)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting above value in (i), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{(1+\log x)^2}{2}=-\tan ^{-1} y+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \frac{1}{2}+\log x+\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}=-\tan ^{-1} y+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \log x+\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}+\tan ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\sin x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \cos x=4 x, x \in(0, \pi)$. If $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0$, then $y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \sin x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \cos x=4 x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y \cot x=\frac{4 x}{\sin x} \\ & \text { Here, } \mathrm{P}(x)=\cot x, \mathrm{Q}(x)=\frac{4 x}{\sin x} \\ & \text { Integrating factor (I.F. })=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{P}(x) \mathrm{d} x} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{out} x \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\sin x|}=\sin x \\ \therefore \quad & y(\text { I.F. })=\int \mathrm{Q}(\text { I.F. }) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & y(\sin x)=\int \frac{4 x}{\sin x} \times \sin x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{C} \\ \therefore \quad & y \sin x=4 \int x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & y=\frac{2 x^2+C}{\sin x} \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2+C}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi^2}{2}+C=0 \\ & \Rightarrow C=\frac{-\pi^2}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow y=\frac{2 x^2-\frac{\pi^2}{2}}{\sin x}\\ &\text { is the solution of the given differential equation. }\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) & =\frac{2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)^2-\frac{\pi^2}{2}}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)} \\ & =\frac{\frac{2 \pi^2}{36}-\frac{\pi^2}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{-8}{9} \pi^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Given that the slope of the tangent to a curve $y=y(x)$ at any point $(x, y)$ is $\frac{2 y}{x^2}$. If the curve passes through the centre of the circle $x^2+y^2-2 x-2 y=0$, then its equation is
explanation:
Equation of the given circle is

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-2 x-2 y=0 \\ \therefore \quad & x^2-2 x+1+y^2-2 y+1=2 \\ \therefore \quad & (x-1)^2+(y-1)^2=2 \\ \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Centre of the circle is $(1,1)$

Now, slope of the given tangent is $\frac{2 y}{x^2}$

i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{2 y}{x^2}$

$\therefore \quad$ Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\int \frac{1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y=2 \int x^{-2} \mathrm{~d} x$$

$\therefore \quad \log |y|=\frac{-2}{x}+c\quad\text{... (i)}$

At $(1,1)$, we get

$\log 1=-2+c$

$\therefore \quad c=2$

$\therefore \quad$ Required equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \log |y|=\frac{-2}{x}+2 \\ & \text { i.e., } x \log |y|=2(x-1) \end{aligned}$$

content: A wet substance in the open air loses its moisture at a rate proportional to the moisture content. If a sheet hung in the open air loses half its moisture during the first hour, then the time t , in which $99 \%$ of the moisture will be lost, is
explanation:
Let $y$ be the amount of moisture at time $t$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\alpha y \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{y}=-\alpha \mathrm{dt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \int \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y}=-\alpha \int \mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \log y=-\alpha \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c} \quad\text{..... (i)}\\ & \text { when } \mathrm{t}=0, y=1 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ From (i), we get

$$c=0\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

when $\mathrm{t}=1, y=0.5$

$\therefore \quad$ From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\log (0.5)=-\alpha$$

$\therefore \quad \alpha=\log 2\quad\text{.... (iii)}$

$\therefore \quad$ From (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

$$\log y=-(\log 2) \mathrm{t}$$

$\therefore \quad$ When $99 \%$ of the moisture will be lost,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & y=0.01 \\ \therefore & \log (0.01)=-(\log 2) \mathrm{t} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log 10}{\log 2} \end{array}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $x \cos y \mathrm{~d} y=\left(x \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \log x+\mathrm{e}^x\right) \mathrm{d} x$ is given by

options.content: $\sin y=\mathrm{e}^x+\operatorname{clog} x$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\sin y=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \log x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\mathrm{e}^x \sin y=\log x+\mathrm{c}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\sin y=\mathrm{ce}^x+\log x$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x \cos y \mathrm{~d} y=\left(x \mathrm{e}^x \log x+\mathrm{e}^x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \Rightarrow \cos y \mathrm{~d} y=\mathrm{e}^x\left(\log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get $\sin y=\mathrm{e}^x \log x+\mathrm{c}$

content: If order and degree of the differential equation $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)^5+4 \frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}\right)^5}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^3 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^3}\right)}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^3 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^3}=\sin x$, are $m$ and $n$ respectively, then the value of $\left(\mathrm{m}^2+\mathrm{n}^2\right)$ is equal to
explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5+4 \frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5}{\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^3 y}{d x^3}=\sin x \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~d}^3 y}{d x^3}+4\left(\frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^3 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^3}\right)^2=\sin x \frac{\mathrm{~d}^3 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^3} \end{aligned}$$

Here, order $=3$ and degree $=2$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad m=3, n=2 \\ & \therefore \quad m^2+n^2=3^2+2^2=13 \end{aligned}$$

content: If a body cools from $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the room temperature of $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 30 min , then the temperature of a body after one hour is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the temperature of the body at any time ' $t$ '.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto(\theta-30) \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=k(\theta-30) \end{array}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \log (\theta-30)=k t+C \\ & \text { when } t=0, \theta=80^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ \therefore \quad & \log (80-30)=\mathrm{k}(0)+\mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\log 50 \\ \therefore \quad & \log (\theta-30)=\mathrm{kt}+\log 50 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=30, \theta=60 \\ \therefore \quad & \log 30=30 \mathrm{k}+\log 50 \\ & \Rightarrow \log 30-\log 50=30 \mathrm{k} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{30} \log \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \end{array}$$

Equation (i) becomes,

$$\log (\theta-30)=\frac{1}{30} \log \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) t+\log 50$$

when $t=60$ minutes, we have

$$\log (\theta-30)=\frac{1}{30} \log \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \times 60+\log 50$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \log (\theta-30)=2 \log \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)+\log 50 \\ & \log (\theta-30)=\log \left(\frac{9}{25} \times 50\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \theta-30=18 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=48^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation $\left[\frac{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}=\mathrm{kx}$ is of
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & {\left[\frac{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}=k x} \\ \therefore \quad & {\left[\frac{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}\right]^3=(k x)^2} \\ \therefore \quad & {\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^3=(k x)^2\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^3} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Order }=2, \text { Degree }=3 \end{array} $$

content: The differential equation of family of circles, whose centres are on the X -axis and also touch the Y -axis is
explanation:
The system of circles whose centre lies on X -axis and touch Y-axis (i.e., passes through the origin) is

$$x^2+y^2=2 \mathrm{~b} x\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Differentiating w.r.t $x$, we get

$$x+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{b}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

Substituting (ii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2=2\left(x+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right) x \\ & \Rightarrow\left(x^2+y^2\right)^2=4\left(x+y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2 x^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: A body cools according to Newton's law of cooling from $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 15 minutes. If the temperature of the surrounding is $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the temperature of the body after cooling down for one hour is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \theta \text { be the temperature of the body at any time } t \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto(\theta-20) \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}(\theta-20), \mathrm{k}>0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Integrating on both sides, we get } \\ & \log |\theta-20|=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, \theta=100^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \log 80=-\mathrm{k}(0)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 80 \\ \therefore \quad & \log |\theta-20|=-\mathrm{kt}+\log 80 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=15, \theta=60^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \log 40=-15 \mathrm{k}+\log 80 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{-1}{15} \log \frac{1}{2} \end{array}.$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \log |\theta-20|=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{15} \log \frac{1}{2}+\log 80 \ldots[\text { From (i) }] \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=1 \text { hour }=60 \text { minutes, } \\ & \log |\theta-20|=\frac{60}{15} \log \frac{1}{2}+\log 80 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\theta-20}{80}\right)=4 \log \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\theta-20}{80}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=5+20=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $y=y(x)$ is the solution of the differential equation $\left(\frac{5+\mathrm{e}^x}{2+y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{e}^x=0$ satisfying $y(0)=1$, then a value of $y(\log 13)$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{5+\mathrm{e}^x}{2+y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{e}^x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{2+y}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^x}{5+\mathrm{e}^x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$\log |2+y|=-\log \left|5+\mathrm{e}^x\right|+\log |\mathrm{c}|$

$$\Rightarrow \log |2+y|=\log \left|\frac{\mathrm{c}}{5+\mathrm{e}^x}\right|$$

Since $y(0)=1$ i.e., $y=1$ when $x=0$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \log 3 & =\log \left|\frac{c}{6}\right| \\ \Rightarrow 3 & =\frac{c}{6} \\ \Rightarrow c & =18 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \log |2+y|=\log \left|\frac{18}{5+\mathrm{e}^x}\right| \\ & \quad \Rightarrow 2+y=\frac{18}{5+\mathrm{e}^x} \quad\text{...[From (i)]}\\ & \quad \Rightarrow y=\frac{18}{5+\mathrm{e}^x}-2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow y(\log 13) & =\frac{18}{5+\mathrm{e}^{\log 13}}-2 \\ & =\frac{18}{5+13}-2 \\ & =-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $(2+\sin x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+(y+1) \cos x=0$ and $y(0)=1$ then $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & (2+\sin x) \frac{d y}{d x}+(y+1) \cos x=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{y+1} \mathrm{~d} y=\frac{-\cos x}{2+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Integrating on both sides, we get } \\ & \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+c \quad\text{... (i)}\\ & \text { When } x=0, y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2 \log 2=\log 4 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+\log 4 \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=\log \left(\frac{4}{2+\sin x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y+1=\frac{4}{2+\sin x} \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{4}{2+\sin x}-1 \\ & \therefore y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x-y)^2$ when $y(1)=1$ is
explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$\mathrm{\frac{dy}{dx}=(x-y)^2}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

Let $x-y=\mathrm{t}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 1-\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=1-\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \end{aligned}$$

From (i)

$$\begin{aligned} & 1-\frac{d t}{d x}=t^2 \\ & 1-t^2=\frac{d t}{d x} \\ & \therefore \quad d x=\frac{1}{1-t^2} d t \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \mathrm{d} x=\int \frac{1}{1-\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt} \\ & x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{1+\mathrm{t}}{1-\mathrm{t}}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{1+x-y}{1-(x-y)}\right|+\mathrm{c} \\ & x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{1+x-y}{1-x+y}\right|+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

But $y(1)=1$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & c=1 \\ & x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{1+x-y}{1-x+y}\right|+1 \\ & x-1=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{1+x-y}{1-x+y}\right| \\ & 2(x-1)=\log \left|\frac{1+x-y}{1-x+y}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow-\log \left|\frac{1-x+y}{1+x-y}\right|=2(x-1) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y(\log y-\log x+1)$, then general solution of this equation is

options.content: $\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\mathrm{cy}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\mathrm{c} x$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\mathrm{cy}$, where c is a constant of integration.
$\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c x$, where $c$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y(\log y-\log x+1) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y}{x}\left[\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+1\right] \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Put } \mathrm{v}=\frac{y}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & y=\mathrm{v} x \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & \text { From (i) } \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}(\log \mathrm{v}+1) \\ & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v} \\ & x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v} \log \mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{1}{v \log v} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \log (\log v)=\log x+c_1 \\ & \log (\log v)=\log x+\log c \text { where, } c_1=\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \log (\log v)=\log (x c) \\ & \Rightarrow \log v=x c \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c x \end{aligned}$$

content: A spherical metal ball at 80$^\circ$C cools in 5 minutes to 60$^\circ$C, in surrounding temperature of 20$^\circ$C, then the temperature of the ball after 20 minutes is approximately
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the temperature of ball at any time ' $t$ '.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto(\theta-20) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}(\theta-20), \mathrm{k}>0 \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get $\log |\theta-20|=-k t+c$

when $\mathrm{t}=0, \theta=80^{\circ}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & c=\log 60 \\ \therefore & \log |\theta-20|=-k t+\log 60\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad \text { When } t=5, \theta=60^{\circ} \\ & \therefore \quad \log 40=-5 k+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 k=\log 60-\log 40 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 k=\log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=\frac{1}{5} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \log |\theta-20|=\frac{-1}{5} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) t+\log 60\quad\text{....[From (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { when } t=20 \\ & \Rightarrow \log |\theta-20|=\frac{-1}{5} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) 20+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow \log |\theta-20|=-4 \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow \log |\theta-20|=\log \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^4+\log 60 \\ & \Rightarrow \log |\theta-20|=\log \left(\frac{16 \times 60}{81}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \log |\theta-20|=\log (11.85) \\ & \Rightarrow \theta-20=11.85 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=11.85+20 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=31.85 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { The temperature of ball after } 20 \text { minutes is }\\ &31.85^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the slope of the tangent of the curve at any point is equal to $$-y+\mathrm{e}^{-x}$$, then the equation of the curve passing through origin is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=-y+\mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+y=\mathrm{e}^{-x} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^x \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Solution of the given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & y \mathrm{e}^x=\int \mathrm{e}^x \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-x} \mathrm{~d} x+c \\ & \Rightarrow y \mathrm{e}^x=\int \mathrm{d} x+c \\ & \Rightarrow y \mathrm{e}^x=x+\mathrm{c} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since the curve passes through $$(0,0)$$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 0=0+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & y \mathrm{e}^x=x \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & \Rightarrow y \mathrm{e}^x-x=0 \end{array}$$

content: If a body cools from $$80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ to $$50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ in the room temperature of $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ in 30 minutes, then the temperature of the body after 1 hour is
explanation:
Let $$\theta$$ be the temperature of the body at any time t.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto(\theta-25) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}(\theta-25), \mathrm{k}>0 \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log |\theta-25|=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=0, \theta=80^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \log 55=0+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 55 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \log |\theta-25|=-\mathrm{kt}+\log 55$$ ..... (i)

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=30, \theta=50^\circ \\ & \log 25=-30 \mathrm{k}+\log 55 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{k}=-\frac{1}{30} \log \frac{5}{11} \\ \therefore \quad & \log |\theta-25|=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{30} \log \frac{5}{11}+\log 55 \quad \text{... [From (i)]} \end{array}$$

When $$\mathrm{t}=1$$ hour $$=60$$ minutes,

$$\begin{aligned} & \log |\theta-25|=\frac{60}{30} \log \frac{5}{11}+\log 55 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\theta-25}{55}\right)=2 \log \left(\frac{5}{11}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\theta-25}{55}=\left(\frac{5}{11}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\theta-25}{55}=\frac{25}{121} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=25+\frac{125}{11}=25+11.36=36.36^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation representing the family of curves $$y^2=2 \mathrm{c}(x+\sqrt{\mathrm{c}})$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a positive parameter, is of
explanation:
$$y^2=2 c(x+\sqrt{c})$$ .... (i)

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 \mathrm{c}$$ ..... (ii)

Substituting (ii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2=2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(x+\sqrt{y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \sqrt{y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(y-2 x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^2=4 y\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)^3 \end{aligned}$$

This is a differential equation of order 1 and degree 3.

content: General solution of the differential equation $$\cos x(1+\cos y) \mathrm{d} x-\sin y(1+\sin x) \mathrm{d} y=0$$ is

options.content: $$(1+\cos x)(1+\sin y)=\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$1+\sin x+\cos y=\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$(1+\sin x)(1+\cos y)=\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}_{\text {is }}$$ constant of integration.
$$1+\sin x \cos y=\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \cos x(1+\cos y) \mathrm{d} x-\sin y(1+\sin x) \mathrm{d} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x-\frac{\sin y}{1+\cos y} \mathrm{~d} y=0 \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log |1+\sin x|+\log |1+\cos y|=\log |\mathrm{c}| \\ & \Rightarrow \log |(1+\sin x)(1+\cos y)|=\log |c| \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\sin x)(1+\cos y)=\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The money invested in a company is compounded continuously. If ₹ 200 invested today becomes ₹ 400 in 6 years, then at the end of 33 years it will become ₹
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Here, Amount }(A)= 400 \\\\ & \text { Principal }(P)= 200, N=6 \text { years } \\\\ & A=P\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^N \\\\ & \Rightarrow 400=200\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^6 \\\\ & \Rightarrow\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^6=2 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 1+\frac{R}{100}=2^{\frac{1}{6}} \\\\ & \begin{aligned} A & =P\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^N \\\\ & =200\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^{33} \\\\ & =200\left(2^{\frac{1}{6}}\right)^{33} \\\\ & =200\left(2^5 \cdot 2^{\frac{1}{2}}\right) \\\\ & =200(32 \sqrt{2}) \\\\ & =6400 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of $$y=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{a} \cos x+\mathrm{b} \sin x)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{a} \cos x+\mathrm{b} \sin x) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{a} \cos x+\mathrm{b} \sin x) +\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b} \cos x-\mathrm{a} \sin x) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b} \cos x-\mathrm{a} \sin x) \\ & \begin{array}{r} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\mathrm{e}^x(\mathrm{~b} \cos x-\mathrm{a} \sin x) \\ +\mathrm{e}^x(-\mathrm{b} \sin x-\mathrm{a} \cos x) \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y\right)-y \quad \text {... [From (i)] } \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{~d} x^2}-2 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 y=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x d y=y(d x+y d y), y(1)=1, y(x)>0$$, then $$y(-3)$$ is
explanation:
$$x d y=y d x+y^2 d y$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \quad -y^2 d y =y d x-x d y \\ \Rightarrow & \quad d y =\frac{y d x-x d y}{-y^2} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad d y =-\left\{d\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)\right\} \end{aligned}$$

On integrating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$y=-\frac{x}{y}+c_1$$

Now, $$y(1)=1$$, If $$x=1, y=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad 1 & =\frac{-1}{1}+c_1 \Rightarrow c_1=2 \\ y & =\frac{-x}{y}+2 \end{aligned}$$

If $$x=-3, y=\frac{3}{y}+2$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad y^2-2 y-3=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & (y-3)(y+1)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & y=3,-1 \end{array}$$

content: The solution of $$(1+x y) y d x+(1-x y) x d y=0$$ is

options.content: $$\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)+\frac{1}{x y}=k$$, where $$k$$ is constant of integration
$$\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{1}{x y}+k$$, where $$k$$ is constant of integration
$$\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)+{x y}=k$$, where $$k$$ is constant of integration
$$\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)={x y}+k$$, where $$k$$ is constant of integration

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { }(1+x y) y d x+(1-x y) x d y=0 \\ & y d x+x d y+x y^2 d x-x^2 y d y=0 \end{aligned}$$

On dividing $$\frac{1}{x^2 y^2}$$ both sides, we get

$$\int \frac{y d x+x d y}{x^2 y^2}+\int \frac{d x}{x}-\int \frac{d y}{y}=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \quad -\frac{1}{x y}+\log x-\log y =k \\ \Rightarrow & \quad -\frac{1}{x y}+\log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right) =k \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right) =\frac{1}{x y}+k \end{aligned}$$

content: A radioactive substance, with initial mass $$m_0$$, has a half-life of $$h$$ days. Then, its initial decay rate is given by
explanation:
Mass of substance at time $$t$$ is $$\mathrm{m}$$

Rate of change of mass $$=\frac{d m}{d t} \propto m$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d m}{d t} & =-k m ; k \rightarrow \text { constant } \\ \int \frac{d m}{m} & =-\int k d t \\ \log m & =-k t+c \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { At } \begin{aligned} t=0, m & =m_0 \\ \log m_0 & =c \\ \log m & =-k t+\log \left(m_0\right) \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

At time $$t=h, m=\frac{1}{2} m_0$$

From Eq. (ii), $$\log \left(\frac{m_0}{2}\right)=-k h+\log \left(m_0\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \quad\log \left(m_0\right)-\log \left(\frac{m_0}{2}\right) =k h \\ \Rightarrow & \quad\log 2 =k h \Rightarrow k=\frac{1}{h} \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

Put value of $$k$$ in Eq. (ii), we get

$$\log m=\left(-\frac{1}{h} \log 2\right) t+\log m_0$$ ..... (iii)

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \log (m)-\log m_0 & =-\frac{t}{h} \log 2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{m}{m_0} & =\log \left(2^{-\frac{t}{h}}\right) \\ \frac{m}{m_0} & =2^{-\frac{t}{h}} \Rightarrow m=2^{-t / h} \cdot m_0 \end{aligned}$$

Initial decay rate

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d m}{d t}=-k m=-\frac{1}{h} \log 2 \times m \\ & \frac{d m}{d t}=-\frac{m}{h} \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

So, initial mass $$=m_0$$ then

$$\frac{d m}{d t}=-\frac{m_0}{h} \log 2$$

content: The solution of the differential equation $$\mathrm{e}^{-x}(y+1) \mathrm{d} y+\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin 2 x\right) y \mathrm{~d} x=0$$ at $$x=0$$, $$y=1$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & e^{-x}(y+1) d y+\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin 2 x\right) y d x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y+1}{y} d y+e^x\left(\cos ^2 x-\sin 2 x\right) d x=0 \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int\left(1+\frac{1}{y}\right) \mathrm{d} y+\int \mathrm{e}^x\left(\cos ^2 x-2 \sin x \cos x\right) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y+\log |y|+\mathrm{e}^x \cos ^2 x=\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { At } x=0, y=1 \\ \therefore \quad & 1+\log 1+\mathrm{e}^0 \cos ^2 0=\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2 \\ \therefore \quad & y+\log |y|+\mathrm{e}^x \cos ^2 x=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Rate of increase of bacteria in a culture is proportional to the number of bacteria present at that instant and it is found that the number doubles in 6 hours. The number of bacteria becomes ________ times at the end of 18 hours.
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{P}_0$$ be the initial population and let the population after $$t$$ years be $$P$$. Then,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d P}{d t}=k P \text {, where } \mathrm{k}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d P}{P}=k d t \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \log P=k t+c \\ & \text { When } t=0, P=P_0 \\ \therefore \quad & \log P_0=0+c \\ \therefore \quad & \operatorname{c}=\log P_0 \\ & \log P=k t+\log P_0 \\ & \Rightarrow \log \frac{P}{P_0}=k t \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { When } t=6 \text { hrs, } P=2 P_0 \\ \therefore \quad & \log \frac{2 P_0}{P_0}=6 k \\ & \Rightarrow k=\frac{\log 2}{6} \\ \therefore \quad & \log \frac{P}{P_0}=\frac{\log 2}{6} t \quad \ldots[\text { From (i)] } \end{array}$$

When $$\mathrm{t}=18 \mathrm{~hrs}$$, we have

$$\begin{aligned} & \log \frac{P}{P_0}=\frac{\log 2}{6} \times 18 \\ & =3 \log 2 \\ \therefore \quad \log \frac{P}{P_0} & =\log 8 \\ \Rightarrow & P=8 P_0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of differential equation $$\mathrm{e}^{\frac{d y}{d x}}=(x+1), y(0)=3$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}}=(x+1) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\log (x+1) \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \int \mathrm{d} y & =\int \log (x+1) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ \Rightarrow y & =x \log (x+1)-\int \frac{x}{x+1} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & =x \log (x+1)-\int \frac{x+1-1}{x+1} \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & =x \log (x+1)-\int\left(1-\frac{1}{x+1}\right) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad y=x \log (x+1)-x+\log (x+1)+\mathrm{c}\quad$$ ... (i)

Since $$y(0)=3$$, i.e., $$y=3$$ when $$x=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 3=0+c \Rightarrow c=3 \\ \therefore & y=x \log (x+1)+\log (x+1)-x+3 \quad ...[\text{From (i)}]\\ \therefore & y=(x+1) \log (x+1)-x+3 \end{array}$$

content: A right circular cone has height $$9 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and radius of base $$5 \mathrm{~cm}$$. It is inverted and water is poured into it. If at any instant, the water level rises at the rate $$\frac{\pi}{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{sec}$$. where $$\mathrm{A}$$ is area of the water surface at that instant, then cone is completely filled in
explanation:
For the conical vessel, $$\mathrm{h}=9 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{r}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Full volume of the vessel,

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{V} & =\frac{1}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~h} \\\\ & =\frac{1}{3} \pi \times 25 \times 9 \\\\ & =75 \pi \mathrm{~cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{9}{5}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad r=\frac{5 h}{9} \\ & \therefore \quad A=\pi r^2=\pi \frac{25 h^2}{81} \end{aligned}$$

According to the given condition,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{dh}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{\pi}{\mathrm{A}}=\pi \frac{81}{\pi 25 \mathrm{~h}^2}=\frac{81}{25 \mathrm{~h}^2} \\\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}^2 \mathrm{dh} & =\frac{81}{25} \mathrm{dt} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{h^3}{3}=\frac{81}{25} t+c_1 \\\\ \therefore \quad h^3 & =\frac{243}{25} t+c, \text { where } c=3 c_1 \end{aligned}$$

Naturally, $$\mathrm{h}=0$$, when $$\mathrm{t}=0$$ and hence, $$\mathrm{c}=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}^3 & =\frac{243}{25} \mathrm{t} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{V} & =\frac{1}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~h} \\\\ & =\frac{1}{3} \pi \frac{25 \mathrm{~h}^2}{81} \mathrm{~h} \\\\ & =\frac{25}{243} \pi \mathrm{h}^3 \\\\ & =\frac{25}{243} \pi \frac{243}{25} \mathrm{t} \quad \text{...[From (i)]}\\\\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{V} & =\pi \mathrm{t} \end{aligned}$$

But volume of vessel, $$\mathrm{V}=75 \pi$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \pi \mathrm{t}=75 \pi \\\\ \therefore & \mathrm{t}=75 \text { seconds. } \end{array}$$

content: The solution of $$\mathrm{e}^{y-x} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y(\sin x+\cos x)}{(1+y \log y)}$$ is

options.content: $$\frac{\mathrm{e}^y}{y}=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\mathrm{e}^y \log y=\mathrm{e}^x \cos x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\mathrm{e}^y \log y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\mathrm{e}^y y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{e}^{y-x} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y(\sin x+\cos x)}{(1+y \log y)} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{e}^y \frac{(1+y \log y)}{y} \mathrm{~d} y=\mathrm{e}^x(\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x \\ \therefore \quad & \int \mathrm{e}^y\left(\log y+\frac{1}{y}\right) \mathrm{d} y=\int \mathrm{e}^x(\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{e}^y \log y=\mathrm{e}^x \sin x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \ldots\left[\because \int \mathrm{e}^x\left(\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)\right) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{e}^x \mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{c}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: Water flows from the base of rectangular tank, of depth 16 meters. The rate of flow of the water is proportional to the square root of depth at any time $$\mathrm{t}$$. If depth is $$4 \mathrm{~m}$$ when $$\mathrm{t}=2$$ hours, then after 3.5 hours the depth (in meters) is
explanation:
Given that $$\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{x}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{a} \sqrt{x}$$, for real number a

$$\therefore \quad \int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\sqrt{x}}=\int \mathrm{a} d \mathrm{t}$$

$$\therefore \quad 2 \sqrt{x}=\mathrm{at}+\mathrm{c}$$ .... (i)

When $$\mathrm{t}=0, x=16$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ (i) $$\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=8$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ (i) becomes $$2 \sqrt{x}=$$ at +8 .... (ii)

When $$\mathrm{t}=2, x=4$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ (ii) $$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-2$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ (ii) becomes $$2 \sqrt{x}=-2 t+8$$ .... (iii)

$$\therefore \quad$$ when $$\mathrm{t}=3.5$$

(iii) $$\Rightarrow x=0.25 \mathrm{~m}$$

content: If $$(2+\sin x) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+(y+1) \cos x=0$$ and $$y(0)=1$$, then $$y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \quad(2+\sin x) \frac{d y}{d x}+(y+1) \cos x=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{y+1} d y=\frac{-\cos x}{2+\sin x} d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+\mathrm{c} \quad. . . \text { (i) } \\ & \text { when } x=0, y=1 \quad \text{... [Given]}\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2 \log 2 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=-\log (2+\sin x)+\log 4 \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y+1)=\log \left(\frac{4}{2+\sin x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y+1=\frac{4}{2+\sin x} \quad \text{... (ii)}\\ & \therefore \quad \text { When } x=\frac{\pi}{2}, \text { (ii) } \Rightarrow y=\frac{4}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The decay rate of radio active material at any time $$t$$ is proportional to its mass at that time. The mass is 27 grams when $$t=0$$. After three hours it was found that 8 grams are left. Then the substance left after one more hour is
explanation:
Let '$$x$$' be the mass of the material at time '$$t$$'.

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k} x,(-\mathrm{ve}$$ sign indicates decay. $$)$$

$$\therefore \quad \int \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{x}=-\mathrm{k} \int \mathrm{dt}$$

$$\therefore \quad \log |x|=-\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}$$

When $$\mathrm{t}=0, x=27$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{c}=\log 27$$

$$\therefore \quad \log |x|=-k t+\log 27$$

When $$\mathrm{t}=3, x=8$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=\log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)$$

When $$\mathrm{t}=4$$, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \log |x|=-4 \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+\log 27 \\ \therefore \quad & \log |x|=\log \left(\frac{16}{3}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & x=\frac{16}{3} \text { grams } \end{array}$$

content: The differential equation $$\cos (x+y) \mathrm{d} y=\mathrm{d} x$$ has the general solution given by
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \cos (x+y) \mathrm{d} y=\mathrm{d} x \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\cos (x+y) \quad \text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Put $$x+y=\mathrm{u}$$ .... (ii)

Differentiating w.r.t. $$y$$, we get

$$\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}+1=\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{d} y}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{d} y}-1$$ ..... (iii)

Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{d} y}-1=\cos \mathrm{u} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{1+\operatorname{cosu}}=\mathrm{d} y \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{du}}{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2}\right)}=\mathrm{d} y \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \int \sec ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2}\right) \mathrm{du}=\int \mathrm{d} y \\ \therefore \quad y & =\tan \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+3$$ and $$y(0)=2$$, then $$y(\log 2)=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=y+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y+3}=\mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{y+3}=\int \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \log (y+3)=x+\mathrm{c} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$y=2 \text { when } x=0\quad$$ ... [Given]

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \log (2+3)=0+c \Rightarrow c=\log 5 \\ \therefore \quad & \log (y+3)=x+\log 5 \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & \Rightarrow y+3=5 \mathrm{e}^x \\ & \Rightarrow y=5 \mathrm{e}^x-3 \\ \therefore \quad & y(\log 2)=5 \mathrm{e}^{\log 2}-3=10-3 \\ & =7 \end{array}$$

content: The solution of $$\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}+\frac{x}{y}=x^2$$ is

options.content: $$\frac{1}{y}=\mathrm{c} x-x \log x$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{1}{x}=\mathrm{c} y-y \log y$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{1}{x}=\mathrm{c} x-x \log y$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$\frac{1}{y}=\mathrm{c} x-y \log x$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}+\frac{x}{y}=x^2 \\ & \frac{1}{x^2} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}+\frac{1}{x y}=1 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\frac{1}{x}=\mathrm{t}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$y$$, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{-1}{x^2} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{d} y} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^2} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{~d} y}=\frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{d} y} \\ \therefore & \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{d} y}+\frac{\mathrm{t}}{y}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{d} y}-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{y}=-1 \\ \therefore & \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{-1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(\frac{1}{y}\right)}=\frac{1}{y} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The solution of the given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{t}(\text { I.F })=\int(-1)(\text { I.F. }) \mathrm{d} y+\mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{t}\left(\frac{1}{y}\right)=\int \frac{-1}{y} \mathrm{~d} y+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{t}{y}=-\log y+c \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{x y}=-\log y+c \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{x}=c y-y \log y \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution of the differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{y}{x}=\sin x$$ is

options.content: $$x y+\cos x=\sin x+\mathrm{c}$$, where c is a constant of integration.
$$x(y+\cos x)=\sin x+\mathrm{c}$$, where c is a constant of integration.
$$y(x+\cos x)=\sin x+c$$, where c is a constant of integration.
$$x y+\sin x=\cos x+\mathrm{c}$$, where c is a constant of integration.

explanation:
For given linear differential equation,

$$\text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log x}=x$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The required solution is

$$y x=\int x \sin x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad y x=-x \cos x+\int \cos x d x \\ & \therefore \quad y x=-x \cos x+\sin x+c \\ & \therefore \quad x(y+\cos x)=\sin x+c \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution of the differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1+y^2}{1+x^2}$$ is

options.content: $$x+y=\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$x-y=\mathrm{c}(x y)$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$x+y=\mathrm{c}(1+x y)$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$y-x=\mathrm{c}(1+x y)$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1+y^2}{1+x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{1+y^2}=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1+x^2} \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{1}{1+y^2} d y-\int \frac{1}{1+x^2} d x=\tan ^{-1} c \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} y-\tan ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1} c \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y-x}{1+x y}\right)=\tan ^{-1} c \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y-x}{1+x y}=c \\ & \Rightarrow y-x=c(1+x y) \end{aligned}$$

content: A curve passes through the point $$\left(1, \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$. Let the slope of the curve at each point $$(x, y)$$ be $$\frac{y}{x}+\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right), x>0$$, then, the equation of the curve is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sec \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Put } y=\mathrm{v} x \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{d} x} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{d} x}=\mathrm{v}+\sec \mathrm{v} \quad \text{... [From (i)]}\\ & \Rightarrow \cos \mathrm{v} d \mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \sin \mathrm{v}=\log (x)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log (x)+\mathrm{c} \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

The curve passes through $$\left(1, \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$.

$$\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\log (1)+c \Rightarrow c=\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation (ii) becomes

$$\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log (x)+\frac{1}{2}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{3 x^2}{1+x^3}\right) y=\frac{1}{x^3+1}$$ is

options.content: $$y\left(1+x^3\right)=x^3+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$y\left(1+x^3\right)=x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$y\left(1+x^3\right)=x^2+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.
$$y\left(1+x^3\right)=2 x+\mathrm{c}$$, where $$\mathrm{c}$$ is a constant of integration.

explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(\frac{3 x^2}{1+x^3}\right) y=\frac{1}{x^3+1} \\ & \text { Here, } \mathrm{P}=\frac{3 x^2}{1+x^3}, \mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{x^3+1} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { I.F. }=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{3 x^2}{1+x^3} \mathrm{~d} x}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+x^3\right)}=\left(1+x^3\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Solution of the given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & y\left(1+x^3\right)=\int \frac{1}{1+x^3} \cdot\left(1+x^3\right) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow y\left(1+x^3\right)=x+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all circles, passing through the origin and having their centres on the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, is
explanation:
The system of circles which passes through origin and whose centre lies on $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis is

$$x^2+y^2-2 b x=0$$ .... (i)

Differentiating w.r.t $$x$$, we get

$$2 x+2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 \mathrm{~b}$$ ..... (ii)

Substituting (ii) in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-2 x^2-2 x y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2-x^2-2 x y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2=x^2+2 x y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \end{aligned}$$

content: The population $$\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{t})$$ at time $$\mathrm{t}$$ of certain species follows the differential equation $$\frac{d P}{d t}=0.5 P-450$$. If $$P(0)=850$$, then the time at which population becomes zero is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given differential equation is } \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0.5 \mathrm{P}-450 \\ & =\frac{P}{2}-\frac{900}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}-900}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{2 \mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}-900}=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \text { Integrating on both sides, we get } \\ & 2 \log |\mathrm{P}-900|=\mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \mathrm{P}(0)=850 \text { i.e., } \mathrm{P}=850 \text { when } \mathrm{t}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad c=2 \log 50 \\ & \therefore \quad 2 \log |\mathrm{P}-900|=\mathrm{t}+2 \log 50 \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{P}=0 \text {, } \\ & 2 \log 900=\mathrm{t}+2 \log 50 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=2(\log 900-\log 50) \\ & =2 \log \frac{900}{50}=2 \log 18 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{y}$$ determines a family of circles with

options.content: variable radii and fixed centre at $$(0,1)$$.
variable radii and fixed centre at $$(0,-1)$$.
fixed radius of 1 unit and variable centre along the $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis.
fixed radius of 1 unit and variable centre along the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis.

explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{y} \\ \therefore \quad & \int \frac{y}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \mathrm{~d} y=\int 1 \mathrm{~d} x \\ \therefore \quad & -\sqrt{1-y^2}=x+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & (x+\mathrm{c})^2=1-y^2 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad(x+c)^2+y^2=1$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Radius is fixed, which is 1 and the centre is $$(-c, 0)$$ which is a variable centre on the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis.

content: General solution of the differential equation $$\cos x(1+\cos y) \mathrm{d} x-\sin y(1+\sin x) \mathrm{d} y=0$$ is
explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos x(1+\cos y) \mathrm{d} x-\sin y(1+\sin x) \mathrm{d} y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos x(1+\cos y) \mathrm{d} x=\sin y(1+\sin x) \mathrm{d} y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\sin y}{1+\cos y} \mathrm{~d} y \end{aligned}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\sin y}{1+\cos y} \mathrm{~d} y+\log |\mathrm{c}| \\ & \Rightarrow \log |1+\sin x|=-\log |1+\cos y|+\log |\mathrm{c}| \\ & \Rightarrow \log |1+\sin x|+\log |1+\cos y|=\log |\mathrm{c}| \\ & \Rightarrow \log |(1+\sin x)(1+\cos y)|=\log |\mathrm{c}| \\ & \Rightarrow(1+\sin x)(1+\cos y)=\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: General solution of the differential equation $$\log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=a x+b y$$ is

options.content: $$a e^{b y}+b e^{a x}=c_1$$, where $$c_1$$ is a constant.
$$a e^{-b y}+b^{-a x}=c_1$$, where $$c_1$$ is a constant.
$$a e^{-b y}+b e^{a x}=c_1$$, where $$c_1$$ is a constant.
$$a e^{b y}+b e^{-a x}=c_1$$, where $$c_1$$ is a constant.

explanation:
Given differential equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & \log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=a x+b y \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a x+b y} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x} & =e^{a x} \cdot e^{b y} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{e}^{b y}}=\mathrm{e}^{a x} \cdot \mathrm{d} x \\ & \mathrm{e}^{-b y} \mathrm{~d} y-\mathrm{e}^{a x} \mathrm{~d} x=0 \end{array}$$

Integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \int e^{-b y} d y-\int e^{a x} d x=0 \\ & \frac{e^{-b y}}{-b}-\frac{e^{a x}}{a}+c=0 \\ & \text { i.e., } \frac{e^{-b y}}{b}+\frac{e^{a x}}{a}=c \\ & a e^{-b y}+b e^{a x}=a b c \\ & a e^{-b y}+b e^{a x}=c_1, \text { where } c_1=a b c \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all circles which pass through the origin and whose centres lie on $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis is
explanation:
Circle passes through origin and centre lie on Y-axis.

Let $$(0, k)$$ be centre and '$$k$$' be radius

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of circle is

$$\begin{aligned} & (x-0)^2+(y-\mathrm{k})^2=\mathrm{k}^2 \\ & x^2+y^2-2 y \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{k}^2=\mathrm{k}^2 \\ & x^2+y^2-2 \mathrm{k} y=0 \\ & x^2+y^2=2 \mathrm{k} y \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \frac{x^2+y^2}{2 y}=\mathrm{k} \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating equation (i) with respect to $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x+2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 \mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \\ & 2 x+2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-2 \mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & 2 x+2(y-\mathrm{k}) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & 2 x+2\left[y-\left(\frac{x^2+y^2}{2 y}\right)\right] \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \quad \text{...[From (ii)]}\\ & 2 x+2\left[\frac{2 y^2-x^2-y^2}{2 y}\right] \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & 2 x+\left(\frac{y^2-x^2}{y}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & 2 x y+\left(y^2-x^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0 \\ & \text { i.e. }\left(x^2-y^2\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-2 x y=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all parabolas, whose axes are parallel to $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis, is
explanation:
Parabola whose axes are parallel to $$\mathrm{Y}$$-axis. Vertex is not $$(0,0)$$

Equation becomes

$$(x-\mathrm{h})^2=4 \mathrm{~b}(y-\mathrm{k}) $$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2(x-h)=4 b\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)$$

Again differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2=4 b\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)$$

Again differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} 0=4 b\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}=0 \\ \text { i.e., } y_3=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$\left(1+y^2\right) \mathrm{d} x-x y \mathrm{~d} y=0$$ at $$x=1, y=0$$, represents
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \left(1+y^2\right) \mathrm{d} x-x y \mathrm{~d} y=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \left(1+y^2\right) \mathrm{d} x=x y \mathrm{~d} y \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{y \mathrm{~d} y}{1+y^2} \end{array}$$

Integrating both sides, we get
