$$\begin{array}{ll} & \int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x=\int \frac{y}{1+y^2} \mathrm{~d} y \\ & \log x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+y^2\right)+\mathrm{c} \\ & \text { At } x=1, y=0 \quad \text{.... [Given]}\\ \therefore \quad & 0=\frac{1}{2} \log (1+0)+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{c}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \log x=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+y^2\right) \\ \therefore \quad & x^2=1+y^2 \\ \therefore \quad & x^2-y^2=1, \end{array}$$

Which is a rectangular hyperbola.

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 x+y}{x-y}$$ is (where $$C$$ is a constant of integration.)
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \because \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3 x+y}{x-y} \quad \therefore \text { put } y=v x \\ & \therefore v+x \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3+v}{1-v} \quad \therefore x \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3+v}{1-v}-v \\ & \therefore x \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3+v-v+v^2}{1-v} \quad \therefore x \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{3+v^2}{1-v} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{1-v}{3+v^2} \mathrm{~d} v=\int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} v \\ & \therefore \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}^2+v^2} \mathrm{~d} v-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v}{3+v^2} \mathrm{~d} v=\log x \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{v}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \log \left(3+v^2\right)=\log x+C \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x \sqrt{3}}\right)-\log \left(\frac{y^2+3 x^2}{x^2}\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \quad=\log x+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{y}$$ determines a family of circles with

options.content: fixed radius of 1 unit and variable centres along the $$X$$-axis
fixed radius of 1 unit and variable centres along the $$Y$$-axis
variable radii and a fixed centre at $$(0,1)$$
variable radii and a fixed centre at $$(0,-1)$$

explanation:
$$\text { D.E. : } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{y} \quad \therefore \frac{y \mathrm{~d} y}{\sqrt{1-y^2}}=\mathrm{d} x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \int \frac{-2 y}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \mathrm{~d} y=-2 \int \mathrm{d} x \\ & \therefore 2 \sqrt{1-y^2}=-2 x+2 c \\ & \therefore \sqrt{1-y^2}=-x+c \\ & \therefore \text { Sq. }: 1-y^2=x^2-2 c x+c^2 \\ & \therefore x^2+y^2-2 c x+\left(c^2-1\right)=0 \\ & \therefore 2 g=-2 c, 2 f=0, k=c^2-1 \\ & \therefore C \equiv(-g,-f) \equiv(c, 0), \\ & r=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-k}=\sqrt{c^2+0^2-\left(c^2-1\right)}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore r=1$$, fixed; $$C \equiv(c, 0)$$, moves on $$X$$-axis as $$c$$ changes.

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$\left(1+e^{2 x}\right) d y+e^x\left(1+y^2\right) d x=0$$ at $$x=0$$ and y = 1 is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { (1+e } \left.e^{2 x}\right) d y+e^x\left(1+y^2\right) d x=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{1+y^2}+\frac{e^x}{\left(1+e^{2 x}\right)} d x=0 \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{1+y^2}=-\int \frac{e^x}{1+e^{2 x}} d x \end{aligned}$$

Put $$e^x=t \Rightarrow e^x d x=d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{1+y^2}=-\int \frac{d t}{1+t^2} \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}(y)=-\tan ^{-1}(t)+c \\ & \therefore \tan ^{-1}(y)+\tan ^{-1}\left(e^x\right)=c \\ & \text { We have } \mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{y}=1 \end{aligned}$$

We have $$x=0, y=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \tan ^{-1}(1)+\tan ^{-1}\left(e^{\circ}\right)=c \quad \Rightarrow c=2 \tan ^{-1}(1)=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \therefore \tan ^{-1} y+\tan ^{-1} e^x=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The order and degree of the differential equation $$\sqrt{\frac{d y}{d x}}-4 \frac{d y}{d x}-7 x=0$$ are respectively.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{\frac{d y}{d x}}-4 \frac{d y}{d x}-7 x=0 \\ & \therefore \sqrt{\frac{d y}{d x}}=4 \frac{d y}{d x}+7 x \text { and squaring both sides, we get } \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=16\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+56 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+49 x^2 \\ & \therefore \text { Order }=1 \text {, degree }=2. \end{aligned}$$

content: A population P grew at the rate given by the equation $$\frac{dP}{dt}=0.5P$$, then the population will become double in
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d P}{d t}=0.05 \mathrm{P} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d P}{0.05 p}=\int d t \Rightarrow 20 \int \frac{d P}{P}=\int d t \\ & \therefore 20 \log |P|=t+c \end{aligned}$$

When $$t=0, P=P \Rightarrow c=20 \log |P|$$

$$\therefore 20 \log |\mathrm{P}|=\mathrm{t}+20 \log |\mathrm{P}|$$

When population doubles, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{t} & =20 \log |2 \mathrm{P}|-20 \log |\mathrm{P}|=20[\log |2 \mathrm{P}|-\log |\mathrm{P}|]=20\left[\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}\right|\right] \\ & =20(\log 2) \text { years } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all parabolas whose axis is $$y$$-axis, is
explanation:
Differential Equation of parabolas whose axis is $$\mathrm{Y}$$ axis, is Let the vertex be $$(0, k)$$.

$$(x-0)^2=4b(y-k)$$ .... (1)

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore x^2=4 b y-4 b k \\ & \therefore 2 x=4 b \frac{d y}{d x}-0 \Rightarrow b=\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting value of b in eq. (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2=4\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}(y-k) \\ & \therefore x^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2 x(y-k) \\ & \therefore x^2\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)(2 x)=2 x \frac{d y}{d x}+2(y-k) \\ & \therefore y-k=\frac{x^2\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}{2} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

Substituting value of $$(y-k)$$ in eq. (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Substituting value of }(y-k) \text { in eq. } \\ & \qquad \begin{array}{c} x^2=4\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\left[\frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}\right]\left[\frac{x^2\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}{2}\right] \\ \therefore x^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=x^3\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right) \Rightarrow x\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\frac{y}{x}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\frac{y}{x} \\ & \text { Put } \frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=x v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d v}{d x}+v \\ & \therefore v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\tan v+v \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\tan v \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d v}{\tan v}=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \log |\sin v|=\log |x|+\log |c| \Rightarrow \sin v \quad=x c \\ & \therefore \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=x c \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$ \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1} $$ when $$ \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{3} $$ and $$ y=\frac{1}{3} $$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1} \\ & \text { Put } x+y=v \Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d v}{d x}-1=\frac{v+1}{v-1} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v+1}{v-1}+1=\frac{2 v}{v-1} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{(v-1) d v}{2 v}=\int d x \\ & \therefore \int \frac{1}{2} d v-\int \frac{1}{2 v} d v=\int d x \Rightarrow \frac{v}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \log |v|=x+c \\ & \therefore \frac{x+y}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \log |x+y|=x+c \end{aligned}$$

We have $$\mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{3}, \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{3}$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \log |1|=\frac{2}{3}+c \quad \Rightarrow c=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{-1}{6} \\ & \therefore \frac{x+y}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \log |x+y|=x-\frac{1}{6} \end{aligned}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 , we get

$$\therefore(x+y)-\log |x+y|=2 x-\frac{2}{6} \Rightarrow \cdot y-x+\frac{1}{3}=\log |x+y|$$

content: The order of the differential equation whose solution is $$y=a \cos x+b \sin x+c e^{-x}$$ is
explanation:
$$y=a \cos x+b \sin x+c e^{-x}$$

Since the equation has 3 arbitrary constants, the order of differential equation is 3.

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$(2 y-1) d x-(2 x+3) d y=0$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & (2 y-1) d x-(2 x+3) d y=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(2 y-1)}{(2 x+3)} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{2 y-1}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x+3} \\ & \quad \frac{\log |2 y-1|}{2}=\frac{\log |2 x+3|}{2}+\log c_1 \Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{2 x+3}{2 y-1}\right|=-\log c_1=\log c \\ & \therefore \frac{2 x+3}{2 y-1}=c \end{aligned} $$

content: The differential equation of the family of parabolas with focus at the origin and the $$X$$-axis a axis, is

options.content: $$y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0$$
$$y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+4 x \frac{d y}{d x}=4 x y$$
$$y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+y=2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}$$

explanation:
Equation of parabola is $$y^2=4 a(x+a)$$.

$$\therefore 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=4 a \quad \Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{y \frac{d y}{d x}}{2}$$

Substituting value of '$$a$$', we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2=4\left(\frac{y \frac{d y}{d x}}{2}\right)\left[x+\left(\frac{y \frac{d y}{d x}}{2}\right)\right]=\left(2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(\frac{2 x+y \frac{d y}{d x}}{2}\right) \\ & 2 y^2=\left(2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(2 x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=4 x y \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \\ & \therefore y=2 x \frac{d y}{d x}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Radium decomposes at the rate proportional to the amount present at any time. If $$\mathrm{P} \%$$ of amount disappears in one year, then amount of radium left after 2 years is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{P} \%$$ amount disappears in one year.

Let initial amount of radium $$=\mathrm{x}_0$$.

$$\therefore$$ Amount left after 1 year $$=\mathrm{x}_0-\frac{\mathrm{P}}{100} \times \mathrm{x}_0=\mathrm{x}_0\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{P}}{100}\right)$$.

Amount left after 2 years

$$\begin{aligned} & =x_0\left(1-\frac{P}{100}\right)-\frac{P}{100} \times x_0\left(1-\frac{P}{100}\right) \\ & =x_0\left(1-\frac{P}{100}\right)\left(1-\frac{P}{100}\right)=x_0\left(1-\frac{P}{100}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation obtained by eliminating A and B from $$y=A \cos \omega t+B \sin \omega t$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=A \cos \omega t+B \sin \omega t \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{A} \omega \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{B} \omega \cos \omega \mathrm{t} \\ & \frac{d^2 y}{d t^2}=-A \omega^2 \cos \omega t-B \omega^2 \sin \omega t=-\omega^2(A \cos \omega t+B \sin \omega t)=-\omega^2 y \\ & \therefore \frac{d^2 y}{d t^2}+\omega^2 y=0 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$y(1+\log x) \frac{d x}{d y}-x \log x=0$$ when $$x=e, y=e^2$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y(1+\log x) \frac{d x}{d y}-x \log x=0 \\ & \therefore \operatorname{y}(1+\log x) \frac{d x}{d y}=x \log x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(1+\log x)}{x \log x} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \frac{1+\log x}{x \log x} d x \\ & \therefore \log |y|=\log |x \log x|+\log c \\ & \text { We haye } x=e, y=e^2 \\ & \therefore \log \left|e^2\right|=\log |e \log e|+\log c \\ & 2=1+\log c \quad \Rightarrow \log c=1=\log e \\ & \therefore \log |y|=\log |x \log x|+\log e \\ & y = e x \log x \end{aligned}$$

content: The order and degree of the differential equation $$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\sqrt{\frac{d y}{d x}}$$ are respectively
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\sqrt{\frac{d y}{d x}} \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^2=\frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \therefore \text { order }=2 \text { and degree }=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\cos (x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \cos (x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=1 \\ & \text { Put } x+y=V \Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d V}{d x} \\ & \therefore \cos V\left(\frac{d V}{d x}-1\right)=1 \Rightarrow \cos V\left(\frac{d V}{d x}\right)=1+\cos V \\ & \therefore \int \frac{\cos V}{1+\cos V} d V=\int d x \\ & \therefore \int\left[\frac{1+\cos V}{1+\cos V}-\frac{1}{1+\cos V}\right] d V=\int d x \Rightarrow \int d V-\frac{1}{2} \int \sec ^2 \frac{V}{2} d V=\int d x \\ & \therefore V-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\tan \left(\frac{V}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}=x+c \Rightarrow V-\tan \left(\frac{V}{2}\right)=x+c \Rightarrow x+y-\tan \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=x+c \\ & y=\tan \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)+c \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^2+y+1}{x^2+x+1}=0$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y^2+y+1}{x^2+x-1}=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{y^2+y+1}{x^2+x-1}\right) \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{y^2+y+1}=-\int \frac{d x}{x^2+x+1} \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y^2+y+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4}}=-\int \frac{d x}{x^2+x+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\int \frac{d y}{\left(y+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}=-\int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}\right]=\frac{-1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{x+\frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}\right]+C_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{(2 y+1)}{\sqrt{3}}+\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}{1-\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\left(\frac{2 x+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}\right]=C_1 \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{2(x+y+1)}{\sqrt{3}}}{\frac{3-(4 y x+2 x+2 y+1)}{3}}\right]=C_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2(x+y+1)}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{3}{2(1-2 x y-x-y)}\right]=C_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{1-2 x y-x-y}\right]=C_1 \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{1-x-y-2 x y}\right]=C_2 \quad \ldots\left[\text { where } C_2=\frac{\sqrt{3} C_1}{2}\right] \\ & \therefore \frac{\sqrt{3}(x+y+1)}{1-x-y-2 x y}=\tan C_2 \Rightarrow \frac{x+y+1}{1-x-y-2 x y}=\frac{\tan C_2}{\sqrt{3}}=c \quad \text{.... (say)}\\ & \therefore x+y+1=c(1-x-y-2 x y) \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{x \log x}{t}$$ is
explanation:
To find the general solution of the differential equation

$$\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{x \log x}{t}$$,

we will follow these steps:

First, separate the variables. Rewrite the given differential equation as:

$$\frac{1}{x \log x} \frac{d x}{d t} = \frac{1}{t}$$.

Now, separate the variables:

$$\frac{1}{x \log x} \, dx = \frac{1}{t} \, dt$$.

Integrate both sides:

$$\int \frac{1}{x \log x} \, dx = \int \frac{1}{t} \, dt$$.

For the left-hand side, let $ u = \log x $. Therefore, $ du = \frac{1}{x} dx $. Substitute $ u $ and $ du $ into the integral:

$$\int \frac{1}{x \log x} \, dx = \int \frac{1}{u} \, du = \log u + C_1 = \log (\log x) + C_1$$.

For the right-hand side, the integral is straightforward:

$$\int \frac{1}{t} \, dt = \log t + C_2$$.

Now, combine the results:

$$\log (\log x) + C_1 = \log t + C_2$$.

To simplify the constants, we can combine them into a single arbitrary constant, say $ C $:

$$\log (\log x) = \log t + C$$.

Exponentiate both sides to eliminate the logarithms:

$$\log x = t e^C = c t$$.

We obtain the general solution:

$$\log x = c t.$$

Therefore, exponentiating both sides yields:

$$x = e^{c t}.$$

So the correct option is:

Option D

content: The particular solution of differential equation $$(x+y) d y+(x-y) d x=0$$ at $$x=y=1$$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & (x+y) d y+(x-y) d x=0 \\ & \text { We have } \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{(x-y)}{x+y} \\ & \text { Put } y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \therefore v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-(x-v x)}{x+v x}=\frac{-x(1-v)}{x(1+v)}=\frac{-(1-v)}{1+v} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1+v}{1+v}-v=\frac{-1+v-v-v^2}{1+v} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-\left(1+v^2\right)}{1+v} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{(1+v)}{1+v^2} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x} \Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{1+v^2}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^2} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \tan ^{-1}(v)+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+v^2\right|=-\log x+\log c_1 \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right|=-\log x+\log c_1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\log \left|\frac{x^2+y^2}{x^2}\right|+2 \log x=\log c \quad \ldots\left[\because \log c=2 \log c_i\right] \\ & \therefore 2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\log \left|\frac{x^2+y^2}{x^2} \times x^2\right|=\log c \Rightarrow 2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\log \left|x^2+y^2\right|=\log c \end{aligned} $$

When $$x=y=1$$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \tan ^{-1}(1)+\log |2| \quad=\log c \quad \Rightarrow \log c=2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\log 2 \\ & \therefore \log \left|x^2+y^2\right|=\frac{\pi}{2}+\log 2-2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{x^2+y^2}{2}\right|=\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \end{aligned} $$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=2^{y-x}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=2^{y-x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2^y}{2^x} \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{2^y}=\int \frac{d x}{2^x} \\ & \therefore \int 2^{-y} d y=\int 2^{-x} d x \Rightarrow \frac{2^{-y}}{-\ell \operatorname{n} 2}=\frac{2^{-x}}{-\ln 2}+c_1 \Rightarrow \frac{2^{-y}}{\ell n 2}=\frac{2^{-x}}{\ell \operatorname{n} 2}-c_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{\ln 2}\left[2^{-x}-2^{-y}\right]=c_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{2^x}-\frac{1}{2^y}=c \text {, where } c=\left(c_1\right)(\ln 2) \end{aligned}$$

content: If the surrounding air is kept at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ and a body cools from $$80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ to $$50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ in 30 minutes, then temperature of the body after one hour will be
explanation:
By Newton's law of cooling, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto\left(\theta-\theta_0\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right) \quad \Rightarrow \int\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\theta-\theta_0}\right)=\int \mathrm{kdt} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \log \left|\theta-\theta_0\right| \quad=k t+c \quad \text{... (1)}\\ & \text { When } t=0, \theta=80 \text { and } \theta_0=25 \\ & \therefore \log |80-25|=0+c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c=\log |55| \quad \text{... (2)}\\ & \text { When } t=30, \theta=50 \\ & \therefore \log |50-25|=30 \mathrm{k}+\log |55| \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{30} \log \left|\frac{5}{11}\right| \quad \text{... (3)}$$

From (1), (2), (3) we write

$$\log \left|\theta-\theta_0\right|=\frac{1}{30} \log \left|\frac{5}{11}\right| t+\log |55|$$

When $$t=60$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { When } t=60 \text {, we get } \\ & \log \left|\theta-\theta_0\right|=\frac{60}{30} \log \left|\frac{5}{11}\right|+\log |55|=\log \left(\left|\frac{5}{11}\right|\right)^2+\log |55|=\log \left|\frac{25}{121} \times 55\right|=\log \left|\frac{125}{11}\right| \\ & \therefore \theta-25=\frac{125}{11} \Rightarrow \theta=36.36^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content: The degree of the differential equation whose solution is $$y^2=8 a(x+a)$$, is
explanation:
We have $$y^2=8 a x+8 a^2\quad \text{..... (1)}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=8 a \Rightarrow a=\left(\frac{y}{4}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}$$

Substituting value of '$$a$$' in eq. (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2=8\left(\frac{y}{4}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}(x)+8\left[\left(\frac{y}{4}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\right]^2=2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{y^2}{2}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \\ & \therefore 2 y^2=4 x y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+y^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence order $$=1$$, degree $$=2$$

content: A spherical raindrop evaporates at a rate proportional to its surface area. If its radius originally is 3 mm. and 1 hour later has been reduced to 2 mm, then the expression of radius r of the raindrop at any time t is (where 0 $$\le$$ t < 3)
explanation:
We have $$\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto-\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2\right)$$

We know that $$\mathrm{v}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 4 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t} \propto-\left(4 \pi r^2\right) \\ & \therefore 4 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t}=\left(-4 k \pi r^2\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d r}{d t}=-k \\ & \therefore \int d r=-k \int d t \\ & \therefore r=-k t+c \quad \text{.... (1)} \end{aligned}$$

We have $$\mathrm{r}=3$$, when $$\mathrm{t}=0$$

$$\therefore 3=c \Rightarrow r=-k t+3 \quad \text{.... (2)}$$

We have $$\mathrm{r}=2$$, when $$\mathrm{t}=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 2=-k+3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k=1 \quad \text{.... (3)}\\ & \therefore r=-t+3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r=3-t \quad \text{.... [From (1), (2), (3)]} \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all parabolas having vertex at the origin and axis along positive Y-axis is
explanation:
We have $$y^2=4 a x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=4 a \Rightarrow a=\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \therefore y^2=4\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) x \Rightarrow y^2=2 x y \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \therefore 2 x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x^2-x}$$, when $$x=2$$ and $$y=1$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x} \quad=\frac{y+1}{x^2-x} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{y+1}=\int \frac{d x}{x(x-1)} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d y}{y+1}=\int\left[\frac{1}{x-1}-\frac{1}{x}\right] d x \Rightarrow \log |y+1|=\log |x-1|-\log |x|+\log c \end{aligned}$$

We have $$x=2$$ and $$y=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \log |2|=\log |1|-\log |1|+\log c \Rightarrow c=2 \\ & \therefore \log |y+1|=\log |x-1|-\log |x|+\log |2| \\ & \log |y+1|=\log \left|\frac{2(x-1)}{x}\right| \\ & \therefore y+1=\frac{2(x-1)}{x} \Rightarrow x y+x=2 x-2 \Rightarrow x y=x-2 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y} \\ & \text { Put } y=v x \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \therefore v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v} \\ & \therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}-v=\frac{1+v-v+v^2}{1-v}=\frac{1+v^2}{1-v} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{(1-v)}{1+v^2} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{1}{1+v^2} d v-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^2} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \tan ^{-1}(v)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+v^2\right|=\log |x|+c \\ & \therefore \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right|-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^2\right|=c \\ & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left|\frac{x^2+y^2}{x^2}\right|+\log \left|x^2\right|\right]=c \\ & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^2+y^2\right|=c \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the diffrential equation $$y(1+\log x)=\left(\log x^x\right) \frac{d y}{d x}$$, when $$y(e)=e^2$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y(1+\log x)=\left(\log x^x\right) \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{(1+\log x)}{\left(\log x^x\right)}=\int \frac{d y}{y} \end{aligned}$$

We know that $$\frac{d}{d x}\left(\log x^x\right)=(1+\log x) d x$$

$$\therefore \log \left|\log \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x}}\right| \quad=\log |\mathrm{y}|+\log \mathrm{c}$$

We have $$\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{e})=\mathrm{e}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \log \left|\log \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e}}\right|=\log \left|\mathrm{e}^2\right|+\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \log |\mathrm{e} \log \mathrm{e}| \quad=2 \log |\mathrm{e}|+\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore 1 \quad=2+\log \mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \log \mathrm{c}=-1 \\ & \therefore \log \left|\log \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{x}}\right|=\log |\mathrm{y}|-\log \mathrm{e} \\ & \therefore \log |\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}|+\log \mathrm{e} \quad=\log |\mathrm{y}| \\ & \therefore \mathrm{e} \mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of $$\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=x+y$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=x+y \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin (x+y) \\ & \text { Put } x+y=t \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d t}{d x}-1=\sin t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d t}{d x}=1+\sin t \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d t}{1+\sin t}=\int d x\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \int \frac{(1-\sin t) d t}{1-\sin ^2 t}=\int d x \Rightarrow \int \frac{1-\sin t}{\cos ^2 t} d t=\int d x \Rightarrow \int\left(\sec ^2 t-\sec t \tan t\right) d t=\int d x \\ & \therefore \tan t-\sec t=x+c \\ & \therefore \tan (x+y)-\sec (x+y)=x+c\end{aligned}$$

content: Solution of the differential equation $$\mathrm{y'=\frac{(x^2+y^2)}{xy}}$$, where y(1) = $$-$$2 is given by
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^2+y^2}{x y}=\frac{x}{y}+\frac{y}{x} \\ & \text { Let } \frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{v} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{v-x\left(\frac{d v}{d x}\right)}{v^2} \\ & \therefore \frac{v-x\left(\frac{d v}{d x}\right)}{v^2}=v+\frac{1}{v} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\left(\frac{x}{v^2}\right) \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\frac{1}{v}\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \int \frac{d v}{3}=-\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \frac{-1}{2 v^2}=-\log x-c \quad \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 v^2}=\log x+c \\ & \therefore \frac{y^2}{2 x^2}=\log x+c \quad \Rightarrow y^2=x^2 \log x^2+2 x^2 c \end{aligned}$$

We have $$y(1)=-2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 4=0+2 c \Rightarrow c=2 \\ & \therefore y^2=x^2 \log x^2+4 x^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all family of lines $$y=m x+\frac{4}{m}$$ obtained by eliminating the arbitrary constant $$\mathrm{m}$$ is
explanation:
y = mx + $$\frac{4}{m}$$ ..... (1)

$$\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=m$$

Substituting value of $$m$$ in equation (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) x+\frac{4}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)} \\ & \therefore y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 x+4 \end{aligned}$$

content:
$$\text{I} : y^{\prime}=\frac{y+x}{x} ; \quad \text { II }: y^{\prime}=\frac{x^2+y}{x^3} ; \quad \text { III }: y^{\prime}=\frac{2 x y}{y^2-x^2}$$

S1 : Differential equations given by I and II are homogeneous differential equations.

S2 : Differential equations given by II and III are homogeneous differential equations.

S3 : Differential equations given by I and III are homogeneous differential equations.

options.content: both S1 and S2 are valid

explanation:
We will check all 3 equations.

I $$: \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+x}{x} \quad \Rightarrow$$ All terms have same degree equal to 1 .

II $$: \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^2+y}{y^3} \quad \Rightarrow$$ Here all terms have different degrees.

III $$: \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y}{y^2-x^2} \Rightarrow$$ All terms have same degree equal to 2 .

content: The differential equation of the family of circles touching $$y$$-axis at the origin is
explanation:
Since circles touch $$\mathrm{Y}$$ axis at origin, the centres of the circles lie on $$\mathrm{X}$$ axis.

Let centre be $$(h, 0)$$ and radius $$=h$$.

$$\therefore(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{h})^2+(\mathrm{y}-0)^2=\mathrm{h}^2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^2-2 \mathrm{hx}+\mathrm{y}^2=0$$ ..... (1)

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2 x-2 h+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=h$$

Substituting value of $$h$$ in eq. (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2-2\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right) x+y^2=0 \\ \therefore & x^2-2 x^2-2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}+y^2=0 \Rightarrow x^2-y^2+2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation. $$\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d x-\left[\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d y=0$$ is
explanation:
We have $$\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d x-\left[\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d y=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)} \\ & \text{Put} \frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \therefore \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v}{\frac{1}{v} \sin v+\cos v}=\frac{v^2 \cos v}{\sin v+v \cos v} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v \sin v}{\sin v+v \cos v} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{\sin v+v \cos v}{v \sin v} d v=\int \frac{-d x}{x} \Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v} d v+\int \cot v d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ & \therefore \log |v|+\log |\sin x|=-\log |x|+\log k \Rightarrow \log |(v)(\sin v)(x)|=\log k \\ & \therefore y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=k \end{aligned}$$

content: If the half life period of a substance is 5 years, then the total amount of the substance left after 15 years, when initial amount is 64 gms is
explanation:
Half life period $$=5$$ years.

Initial quantity of substance $$=64$$ grams

$$\therefore$$ Quantity left after 5 years $$=\frac{64}{2}=32$$ grams

$$\therefore$$ Quantity left after 10 years $$=\frac{32}{2}=16$$ grams

$$\therefore$$ Quantity left after 15 years $$=\frac{16}{2}=8$$ grams

content: If $$m$$ is order and $$n$$ is degree of the differential equation $$\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5+4 \frac{\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}{\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)}+\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)=x^2$$ then
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have }\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5+\frac{4\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)}{\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)}+\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)=x^2-1 \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^5+4\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)+\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2=\left(x^2-1\right)\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right) \\ & \therefore \text { order }=3, \text { degree }=2\end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\left(3 x y+y^2\right) d x+\left(x^2+x y\right) d y=0$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \left(3 x y+y^2\right) d x+\left(x^2+x y\right) d y=0 \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\left(3 x y+y^2\right)}{\left(x^2+x y\right)} \end{aligned}$$

Put $$y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{\left(3 v x^2+v^2 x^2\right)}{\left(x^2+v^2\right)}=-\frac{3 v+v^2}{1+v} \\ & \therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{3 v+v^2}{1+v}-v=-\frac{\left(2 v^2+4 v\right)}{1+v} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{1+v}{\left(2 v^2+4 v\right)} d v=\int \frac{-1}{x} d x \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{v+1}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \int \frac{2(v+1)}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\log x+\log c_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{4} \log \left(v^2+2 v\right)+\log x=\log c_1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}+\frac{2 y}{x}\right)+\log x=\log c_1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{y^2+2 x y}{x^2}\right)+\log x=\log c_1 \Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{y^2+2 x y}{x^2}\right)+\log x^4=4 \log c_1 \\ & \therefore \log \left[\left(\frac{y^2+2 x y}{x^2}\right)\left(x^4\right)\right]=\log c_1^4 \Rightarrow \log \left[x^2\left(y^2+2 x y\right)\right]=\log c_1^4 \\ & \therefore x^2\left(y^2+2 x y\right)=c \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of family of circles whose centres lie on $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis is
explanation:
Let $$(\mathrm{h}, 0)$$ be the centre of the circle and '$$r$$' be the radius.

Let $$(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{h}-\mathrm{h})^2+(\mathrm{y}-0)^2=\mathrm{r}^2 \Rightarrow \quad(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{h})^2+\mathrm{y}^2=\mathrm{r}^2$$ ....... (1)

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\therefore 2(x-h)(1)+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow h=x+y \frac{d y}{d x}$$

Substituting value of '$$h$$' in eq. (i), we get

$$y^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+y^2=r^2 \Rightarrow y^2\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]=r^2$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2\left(2 \frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)+\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right](2 y) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \therefore y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+1=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$y(1+\log x)\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)-x \log x=0$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{y}(1+\log \mathrm{x})\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dy}}\right)-\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}=0 \\ & \therefore \int \frac{(1+\log \mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}}+\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \therefore \log (\log \mathrm{x})+\log \mathrm{x}=\log \mathrm{y}+\log \mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore \log [\mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}]=\log (\mathrm{yc}) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \log \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{yc} \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+2 y-1}{x+2 y+1}$$ is
explanation:
$$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+2 y-1}{x+2 y+1}$$

Put $$x+2 y=t \Rightarrow 1+2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{d t}{d x}-1\right)}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\left(\frac{d t}{d x}-1\right)}{2}=\frac{t-1}{t+1} \Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}-1=\frac{2 t-2}{t+1} \\ & \therefore \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{2 t-2}{t+1}+1=\frac{3 t-1}{t+1} \\ & \therefore \frac{t+1}{3 t-1} d t=\int d x \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{3(t+1)}{3 t-1} d t=\int d x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{3 t-1+4}{3 t-1} d t=\int d x \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{3} \int d t+\frac{4}{3} \int \frac{d t}{3 t-1}=\int d x \\ & \therefore \frac{t}{3}+\frac{4}{3} \frac{\log |3 t-1|}{3}=x+c_1 \\ & \therefore \frac{x+2 y}{3}+\frac{4}{3} \frac{\log |3(x+2 y)-1|}{3}=x+c_1 \\ & \therefore {3 x+6 y+4 \log |3 x+6 y-1|=9 x+9 c_1}{\therefore} 6(-x+y)+4 \log |3 x+6 y-1|=K\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{m}$$ is order and $$\mathrm{n}$$ is degree of the differential equation $$y=\frac{d p}{d x}+\sqrt{a^2 p^2-b^2}$$, where $$p=\frac{d y}{d x}$$, then the value of $$m+n$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{d p}{d x}+\sqrt{a^2 p^2-b^2}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+\sqrt{a^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2-b^2} \\ & \therefore y-\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\sqrt{a^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2-b^2} \end{aligned}$$

Squaring both sides, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2+\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x}\right)^2-2 y \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=a^2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2-b^2 \\ & \therefore \text { order }=2 \text { and degree }=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\cos x \cdot \sin y d x+\sin x \cdot \cos y d y=0$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \cos x \sin y d x+\sin x \cos y d y=0 \\\\ & \therefore \cos x \sin y d x=-\sin x \cos y d y \\\\ & \therefore \int \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} d x=-\int \frac{\cos y}{\sin y} d y \\\\ & \therefore \log |\sin x|=-\log |\sin y|+c_1 \\\\ & \therefore \log |\sin x|+\log |\sin y|=c_1 \Rightarrow \log [\sin x \sin y]=c_1 \\\\ & \therefore \sin x \sin y=\mathrm{e}^{c_1}=c\end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of an ellipse whose major axis is twice its minor axis, is
explanation:
For the given ellipse, we have $$a=2 b$$

$$\therefore \frac{x^2}{4 b^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow x^2+4 y^2=4 b^2$$

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2 x+8 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x+4 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$$

content: The general solution of $$\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right) \sin \frac{y}{x}=x^3 e^x$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right) \sin \frac{y}{x}=x^3 e^x\quad ..... \text{(i)} \\ & \text { Put } \frac{y}{x}=t \Rightarrow \frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^2}=\frac{d t}{d x} \end{aligned}$$

Hence eq. (i) becomes

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right) \sin \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{x}^3 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right) \sin \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \therefore \int \sin \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt}=\int \mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx} \\ & \therefore-\cos \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}}-\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c} \\ & \therefore-\cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x}-1)+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

content: The population of a city increases at a rate proportional to the population at that time. If the population of the city increase from 20 lakhs to 40 lakhs in 30 years, then after another 15 years the population is
explanation:
We have $$\frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \mathrm{P} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kP}$$

$$\therefore \int \frac{\mathrm{dP}}{\mathrm{P}}=\int \mathrm{kdt} \Rightarrow \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{c}$$

From given data, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \log 20=\mathrm{k}(0)+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\log 20 \\ & \therefore \log \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{kt}+\log 20 \end{aligned}$$

Also $$\log 40=30 \mathrm{k}+\log 20$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \log 40-\log 20=30 \mathrm{k} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{30} \log 2 \\ & \therefore \log \mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{\log 2}{30}\right) \mathrm{t}+\log 20 \end{aligned}$$

When $$t=30+15=45$$,

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \log P & =\left(\frac{\log 2}{30}\right)(45)+\log 20=(\log 2)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+\log 20 \\ & =\log (2)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\log 20=\log (2 \sqrt{2} \times 20) \\ \therefore \quad P & =40 \sqrt{2} \text { lakhs } \end{aligned}$$

content: A differential equation for the temperature 'T' of a hot body as a function of time, when it is placed in a bath which is held at a constant temperature of 32$$^\circ$$ F, is given by (where k is a constant of proportionality)
explanation:
The temperature T of the body will decrease with time.

The body is kept in a bath of temperature 32$$^\circ$$ F.

$$\therefore \mathrm{\frac{dT}{dt}\alpha-(T-32)\Rightarrow \frac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-32)}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1}$$ is given by
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x} \quad=\frac{x+y+1}{x+y-1} \\ & \text { Put } \quad x+y=u \Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x} \\ & \therefore \frac{d u}{d x}-1=\frac{u+1}{u-1} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{u+1}{u-1}+1=\frac{2 u}{u-1} \\ & \therefore\left(\frac{u-1}{u}\right) d u=2 d x \\ & \text { Integrating both sides, we get } \\ & \int d u-\int \frac{d u}{u}=\int 2 d x \\ & \therefore u-\log u=2 x+c \\ & \therefore x+y-\log (x+y)=2 x+c \Rightarrow y=x+\log (x+y)+c\end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $$x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec \left(x^2+y^2\right)$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec \left(x^2+y^2\right)$$

Put $$x^2+y^2=u \Rightarrow 2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad x+y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d u}{d x} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} \frac{d u}{d x}=\sec u \\ & \therefore \int \frac{d u}{\sec u}=\int 2 d x \\ & \therefore \quad \sin u=2 x+c \Rightarrow \sin \left(x^2+y^2\right)=2 x+c\end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all circles which pass through the origin and whose centre lie on Y-axis is
explanation:
Let (0, K) be the centre of the circle.

Since the circle passes through origin, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & (x-0)^2+(y-K)^2=K^2 \\ & \therefore x^2+y^2-2 K y+K^2=K^2 \\ & \therefore x^2+y^2-2 K y=0\quad ... (1) \end{aligned}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-2 K \frac{d y}{d x}=0$$

$$\therefore K=\frac{x+y \frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d y}{d x}}$$ and substituting value of K in (1), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2-2\left(\frac{x+y \frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d y}{d x}}\right) y=0 \\ \therefore & \left(x^2+y^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y-2 y^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \quad \Rightarrow\left(x^2-y^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y \quad=0\end{aligned}$$

content: An ice ball melts at the rate which is proportional to the amount of ice at that instant. Half the quantity of ice melts in 20 minutes, $$x_0$$ is the initial quantity of ice. If after 40 minutes the amount of ice left is $$\mathrm{Kx}_0$$, then $$\mathrm{K}=$$
explanation:
Given that the rate of melting ($\frac{dx}{dt}$) is proportional to the amount of ice ($x$) present at that moment, we can write the differential equation as :

$ \frac{dx}{dt} = -kx $

(Here the negative sign indicates melting or decrease in quantity)

Let's solve this ordinary differential equation :

Separate the variables :

$ \frac{dx}{x} = -k dt $

Now, integrate both sides :

$ \int \frac{dx}{x} = -k \int dt $

This gives :

$ \ln|x| = -kt + C $

Where $C$ is the constant of integration.

Given that half the quantity of ice melts in 20 minutes, we have :

$ x = \frac{x_0}{2} $ when $ t = 20 $

Plug this into our equation to find $C$ :

$ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{2}\right) = -20k + C $

Now, using the initial condition that $ x = x_0 $ when $ t = 0 $ :

$ \ln(x_0) = C $

From these two, we get :

$ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{2}\right) = \ln(x_0) - 20k $

Which gives :

$ k = \frac{\ln 2}{20} $

After 40 minutes, let $ x = Kx_0 $, plug into the equation :

$ \ln(Kx_0) = -\frac{\ln 2}{20} \times 40 + \ln(x_0) $

$ \ln(Kx_0) = -2\ln 2 + \ln(x_0) $

$ \ln(K) = -2\ln 2 $

$ K = e^{-2\ln 2} $

$ K = e^{\ln(2^{-2})} $

$ K = 2^{-2} $

$ K = \frac{1}{4} $

So, the correct answer is :

Option C :

$ K = \frac{1}{4} $

content: The integrating factor of the differential equation $x \frac{d y}{d x}+y \log x=x^2$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x \frac{d y}{d x}+y \log x=x^2 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}+y\left(\frac{\log x}{x}\right)=x \\ I F & =e^{\int \frac{\log x}{x} d x} \\ & =e^{\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}} \\ & =e^{\frac{1}{2} \log x \cdot \log x} \\ & =e^{\log \sqrt{x} \log x} \\ = & e^{\log x \log \sqrt{x}}=x^{\log \sqrt{x}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The rate of disintegration of a radio active element at time $t$ is proportional to its mass, at the time. Then the time during which the original mass of 1.5 gm . Will disintegrate into its mass of 0.5 gm . is proportional to
explanation:
Let $M$ be the mass of radioactive element at any time ' $t$ '

Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d M}{d t}=k M \\ & \frac{d M}{M}=k d t \end{aligned}$$

On integrating, we get

$$\qquad\log M=k t+C$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{When,}\quad t=0,M=1.5 ~\text{gm}\\ & \therefore \quad C=\log (1.5) \\ & \therefore \quad \log M=k t+\log (1.5) \end{aligned}$$

When, $\quad t=t, M=0.5 \mathrm{~gm}$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \therefore \quad & \log (0.5) =k t+\log (1.5) \\ & \log \left(\frac{0.5}{1.5}\right) =k t \Rightarrow \log \frac{1}{3}=k t \\ \Rightarrow & t =(\log 3) k t \end{array}$$

$\therefore$ Time is proportional to $\log 3$

content: The general solution of the differential equation $\left(1+y^2\right)+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$ is
explanation:
Given equation,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \left(1+y^2\right)+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^2}=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^2} \\ \therefore & \quad \text { FF }=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^2} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y} \end{array}$$

Solution of given differential equation is,

$$\begin{aligned} & x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^2} d y+c \\ & x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}}{2}+c=\frac{\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right)^2}{2}+c \end{aligned}$$

content: The order and degree of the differential equation $\left[1+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}\right]^{\frac{5}{3}}=5 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$ are respectively
explanation:
Given, equation

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[1+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}\right]^{\frac{5}{3}}=5 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}} \\ & {\left[1+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}\right]^5=125\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^3} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ order = 2 degree = 3

content: If the population grows at the rate of $8 \%$ per year, then the time taken for the population to be doubled, is (Given $\log 2=0.6912$)
explanation:
Let ' $P$ ' be the population at any time ' $q$ '

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given,}\quad & \frac{d P}{d t}=8 \% \text { of } P \\ & \frac{d P}{P}=\frac{8}{100} d t \end{aligned}$$

On integrating we get,

$$\log P=\frac{8}{100} t+C$$

When, $$t=0, P=P_0$$

$$\begin{array}{lll} \Rightarrow & C & =\log P_0 \\ \therefore & & \log \frac{P}{P_0}=\frac{8}{100} t \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { When, } \quad P=2 P_0 \\ & \therefore \quad \log 2=\frac{8}{100} t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{100}{8} \log 2=\frac{100}{8} \times 0.6912=8.64 \mathrm{yr} \end{aligned}$$

content: The integrating factor of the differential equation $$\sin y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\cos y(1-x \cos y)$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & \sin y \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos y(1-x \cos y) \\ & \tan y \frac{d y}{d x}=1-x \cos y \\ & \tan y \frac{d y}{d x}-1=-x \cos y \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{lr} & \sec y \tan y \frac{d y}{d x}-\sec y=-x \\ \text { Put, } & \sec y=z \\ \therefore & \sec y \tan y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x} \\ \therefore & \frac{d z}{d x}-z=-x \\ \therefore & \text { IF }=e^{\int-d x}=e^{-x} \end{array}$$

content: The order and degree of the differential equation $$\left[1+\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]^3\right]^{\frac{7}{3}}=7 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$$ are respectively.
explanation:
Given differential equation,

$$\begin{aligned} & {\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3\right]^{7 / 3}=7 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}} \\ & \left(1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3\right)^7=7^3\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^3 \end{aligned}$$

Clearly order $$=2$$ degree $$=3$$

content: The rate at which the metal cools in moving air is proportional to the difference of temperatures between the metal and air. If the air temperature is 290 K and the metal temperature drops from 370 K to 330 K in 10 min , then the time required to drop the temperature upto 295 K is
explanation:
Let $$T$$ be temperature of metal at any time $$t$$ and $$T_0$$ is temperature of air

According to given condition,

$$\frac{d T}{d t}=k\left(T-T_0\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & & \frac{d T}{T-T_0} & =k d t \\ \Rightarrow & & \int \frac{d T}{T-T_0} & =k \int d t \\ \Rightarrow & & \log \left(T-T_0\right) & =k t+C \\ \text { Here, } & & T_0 & =290 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \log (T-290)=k t+C$$

When, $$t=0, T=370$$

When, $$t=10, T=330$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad C=\log 80 \\ & \log \left(\frac{T-290}{80}\right)=k t \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & k=\frac{1}{10} \log \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \log \left(\frac{T-290}{80}\right)=\frac{t}{10} \log \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { When, }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \log \frac{1}{16}=\frac{t}{10} \log \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & 10 \times 4 \log \frac{1}{2}=t \log \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & t=40 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content: The micro-organisms double themselves in 3 h. Assuming that the quantity increases at a rate proportional to it self, then the number of times it multiplies themselves in 18 yr is
explanation:
Let the number of micro-organism in initial is $$n$$

Here, $$n$$ will be $$2 n$$ in 3 yr

$$\therefore$$ Number of micro-organism in 18 yr

$$=\left(2^6\right) n=64 n$$

$$\therefore 64$$ time of micro-organism in 18 yr .

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$y\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)=x \log x$$ at $$x=e$$ and $$y=1$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & y \frac{d x}{d y}=x \log x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{d y}{y}=\frac{d x}{x \log x} \end{aligned}$$

On integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \log y=\log (\log x)+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=c \log x \end{aligned}$$

When, $$x=e \text { and } y=1 \Rightarrow c=1$$

$$\begin{array}{r} \therefore \quad y=\log x \\ x=e^y \end{array}$$

content: The differential equation obtained from the function $$y=a(x-a)^2$$ is

options.content: $$8 y^2=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\left[x+\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2$$
$$4 y^2=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\left[x-\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2$$
$$2 y^2=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\left[x-\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2$$
$$8 y^2=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\left[x-\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2$$

explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} y & =a(x-a)^2 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =2 a(x-a) \\ \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 & =4 a^2(x-a)^2 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{y} & =\frac{4 a^2(x-a)^2}{a(x-a)^2}=4 a \\ \therefore \quad a & =\frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{4 y} \end{aligned}$$

Putting, the value of a in Eq. (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} y & =\frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2}{4 y}\left[x-\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 4 y^2 & =\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\left[x-\frac{1}{4 y}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2\right]^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The differential equation of all lines perpendicular to the line $$5 x+2 y+7=0$$ is
explanation:
The given equation of the line is $$5x + 2y + 7 = 0$$. The slope of any line perpendicular to this line can be found by taking the negative reciprocal of the slope of this line. The slope of the given line is $$-\frac{5}{2}$$ since in the form $$y = mx + c$$, where $$m$$ is the slope, comparing it with the given equation, we have $$2y = -5x - 7$$, or $$y = -\frac{5}{2}x - \frac{7}{2}$$, thus $$m = -\frac{5}{2}$$. The slope of the line perpendicular to it would be $$\frac{2}{5}$$, as we take the negative reciprocal of the original slope to find the perpendicular slope.

Now, when we express the differential form of the equation representing lines with this slope, we use the fact that the slope is also the derivative of $$y$$ with respect to $$x$$ (i.e., $$\frac{dy}{dx}$$ for the lines), which should equal the perpendicular slope found above ($$\frac{2}{5}$$). Thus, we set $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{2}{5}$$ and rearranging this in terms of differential forms (multiplying both sides by $$dx$$) we get:

$$5dy = 2dx$$

Therefore, the correct differential equation representing all lines perpendicular to $$5x + 2y + 7 = 0$$ is given by Option B: $$5dy-2dx=0$$.

content: The bacteria increases at the rate proportional to the number of bacteria present. If the original number '$$N$$' doubles in $$4 \mathrm{~h}$$, then the number of bacteria in $$12 \mathrm{~h}$$ will be
explanation:
The given problem describes a classic case of exponential growth, which can be modeled with the equation:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}$$

where:

  • $$N(t)$$ is the number of bacteria at time $$t$$,
  • $$N_0$$ is the initial number of bacteria (here represented as $$N$$),
  • $$k$$ is the rate constant,
  • and $$t$$ is the time in hours.

It's given that the number of bacteria doubles in 4 hours. This means that $$N(4) = 2N_0$$ (or simply, $$2N$$ if we denote $$N_0$$ as $$N$$). We can use this information to solve for $$k$$:

$$2N = N \cdot e^{4k}$$

By simplifying, we get:

$$2 = e^{4k}$$

This implies:

$$\ln(2) = 4k$$

$$k = \frac{\ln(2)}{4}$$

Now, to find the number of bacteria after 12 hours, we use the value of $$k$$ we just found:

$$N(12) = N \cdot e^{12 \cdot \frac{\ln(2)}{4}}$$

This simplifies to:

$$N(12) = N \cdot e^{3\ln(2)}$$

Remember that $$e^{\ln(x)} = x$$, so we can further simplify:

$$N(12) = N \cdot 2^3$$

$$N(12) = N \cdot 8$$

Therefore, the number of bacteria after 12 hours will be $$8N$$. The correct option is Option B: 8N.

content: The rate of decay of certain substance is directly proportional to the amount present at that instant. Initially, there are $$27 \mathrm{~gm}$$ of certain substance and $$3 \mathrm{~h}$$ later it is found that $$8 \mathrm{~gm}$$ are left, then the amount left after one more hour is
explanation:
Let $$N_{g m}$$ be amount present at any time $$t$$

According to given conditions,

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{d N}{d t}=k N \Rightarrow \frac{d N}{N}=k d t \Rightarrow \int \frac{d N}{N}=k \int d t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \log N=k t+C \Rightarrow N=A e^{k t} \\ & \text { When } t=0, N=27 \mathrm{gm} \\ & \therefore \quad A=27 \mathrm{gm} \\ & \quad N=27 e^{k t} \end{aligned}$$

When $$t=3, N=8$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 8=27 e^{3 k} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e^k=\left(\frac{8}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad N=27\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^t \\ & \text { put } \quad t=4 \\ & \therefore \quad N=27\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^4=\frac{27 \times 16}{81}=\frac{16}{3} \\ & N=\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{gm} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The integrating factor of the differential equation $$\left(1+x^2\right) d t=\left(\tan ^{-1} x-t\right) d x$$ is
explanation:
To find the integrating factor of the given differential equation, first, we need to rearrange the equation into the standard linear differential equation form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$$

The given differential equation is:

$$\left(1+x^2\right) d t=\left(\tan ^{-1} x-t\right) d x$$

Rewriting it, we get:

$$\left(1+x^2\right)dt + tdx = \tan^{-1}xdx$$

$$dt + \frac{t}{1+x^2}dx = \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}dx$$

Here, $$P(x) = \frac{1}{1+x^2}$$ and $$Q(x) = \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}$$.

The integrating factor (IF) is calculated using the formula:

$$\text{IF} = e^{\int P(x)dx}$$

Substituting the value of $$P(x)$$:

$$\text{IF} = e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^2} dx}$$

The integral of $$\frac{1}{1+x^2}$$ is $$\tan^{-1}x$$, so we get:

$$\text{IF} = e^{\tan^{-1}x}$$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A

$$e^{\tan ^{-1} x}$$

content: The order of the differential equation of all circles which lie in the first quadrant and touch both the axes is......
explanation:
Equation of the circle touching both axes is $x^2+y^2-2 a x-2 a y+a^2=0$

Here, number of arbitrary constant is one which is equal to the order of its differential equation.

content: The solution of differential equation $\left(x^2+1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(y^2+1\right)=0$ is $\ldots$
explanation:
We have, differential equation

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(x^2+1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(y^2+1\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d x}{x^2+1}=-\frac{d y}{y^2+1} \end{aligned}$$

On the integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} \int \frac{d x}{x^2+1} & =-\int \frac{d y}{y^2+1} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \tan ^{-1} x & =-\tan ^{-1} y+C \\ \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y & =C \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $\log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=x$, when $x=0, y=1$ is ..............
explanation:
We have, differential equations,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & d y=e^x d x \end{array}$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{r} \int d y=\int e^x d x \\ y=e^x+C\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array}$$

On putting $x=0, y=1$ is Eq. (i), we get

$$1=e^0+C \Rightarrow C=0$$

Now, particular solution of the given differential is $y=e^x$

content: The solution of the differential equation $y d x-x d y=x y d x$ is ......
explanation:
We have differential equation $y d x-x d y=x y d x$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{y d x-x d y}{x y}=d x \\ \Rightarrow & d\left(\log \frac{x}{y}\right)=d x \end{array}$$

On integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{gathered} \log \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=x \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=e^x \\ x=y e^x \end{gathered}$$

content: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d \theta}{d t}=-k\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ where $k$ is constant, is .............
explanation:
We have differential equation

$\frac{d \theta}{d t}=-k\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$, where $k$ is constant

$$\Rightarrow \frac{d \theta}{d t}+k \theta=k \theta_0$$

which is linear differential equation in the form of

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q \\ \therefore\quad & I F=e^{\int{k t t}}=e^{k t} \end{aligned}$$

therefore required solution,

$$\begin{aligned} & (\theta)\left(e^{k t}\right)=\int\left(e^{k t} \times k \theta_0\right) d t \\ & \Rightarrow \theta e^{k t}=e^{k t} \theta_0+a \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\theta_0+a e^{-k t} \end{aligned}$$

content: The order of the differential equation of all circles whose radius is 4 , is ...........
explanation:
Let equation of all circles having centre $(a, b)$ and given radius is 4 , is

$$(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=4$$

Here, number of arbitrary constant in two $(a, b)$ so its order is 2.

content: The general solution of $x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$ is .............
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \\ & \text { Put } \quad \frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} \\ & \therefore \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\tan v \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\tan v \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \cot v d v=-\frac{d x}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \log (\sin v)=-\log (x)+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \log (\sin v \cdot x)=\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \cdot x=c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c \end{aligned}$$
